nodes.body: The rowboat scraped onto the sand at first light, and Wren climbed out with a satchel, a flask, and one sheet of blank paper. The mate pointed at the island rising out of the mist and made the rule plain. Fill that paper with a true chart before the ship returns at dusk, or the apprenticeship goes to someone else. Then the oars dipped and the boat pulled away, and Wren stood alone on a shore that no map had ever named. Five ways led inland from the beach. A tangled mangrove coast dripped and steamed off to the left. A green cloud-forest ridge climbed straight ahead. Dark sink-hole caverns breathed cool air from below. A leaning survey tower waited out on the headland. And to the right, wide tidal flats shone like a sheet of glass. Wren took a slow breath and chose.

choices.label: Wade into the mangrove coast.
Climb toward the cloud-forest ridge.
Descend into the sink-hole caverns.
Cross to the ruined survey tower.
Walk out onto the tidal flats.

nodes.body: The mangrove coast was a maze of roots and slow brown water. Herons stood among the trees, still as sticks, waiting for fish to drift past. Wren stepped in with care, testing the mud before each stride, because a wrong step here could swallow a boot to the knee. The morning would not last, and the mud would swallow it too if Wren dithered. To draw anything true, Wren needed a bearing, and three of them offered themselves at once. Upcoast, a fallen log lay across a channel like a slick green bridge. Downcoast, a cluster of oyster rocks broke the shallow water. Straight inland, a hidden creek slid away under the leaves. And close by, a still green lagoon glimmered, quietly asking to be crossed.

choices.label: Follow the log bridge upcoast.
Pick along the oyster rocks downcoast.
Push inland up the hidden creek.
Wade toward the still green lagoon.

nodes.body: The fallen log was slick and furred with green, and it crossed a channel where the tide had already begun to turn. Wren went slowly, arms out for balance, boots gripping the wet bark. Halfway across, something caught the eye. A knot of driftwood was wedged against the log, and its pale wood had been carved with old marks, faint but deliberate. On the far bank a heron rookery clattered and squawked, hundreds of birds packed into the branches. Wren paused, one foot forward, the tide sliding below. The carving might mean something, or it might be nothing. But the water was rising, and a stalled crossing on a greasy log was no place to linger.

choices.label: Stop to copy the carved driftwood.
Hurry across before the tide.

nodes.body: Wren crouched low and studied the driftwood. The marks were a mapmaker's shorthand, arrows and a small compass rose, scratched by someone a long time ago. The wood had greyed around them, so they were older than this morning by many seasons. Two of the arrows pointed clearly, one back toward the sea caves along the shore, one up toward higher ground away from the swamp. It was a message left on purpose, Wren felt sure, a hand reaching across the years. There was time to trust the arrow and move, or time to read the whole thing slowly and be certain of what it said.

choices.label: Trust the arrow toward high ground.
Read the marks slowly for a message.

nodes.body: Wren read the marks slowly, the way the Chart House taught, letting the shorthand settle into sense. It was a message after all. The last strokes spelled a name, Alder, cut small and careful into the grey wood. Below the name ran a promise, that a full chart of the island had been left at a camp up on the ridge, waiting for anyone who came after. The arrow up the hill was clear and sure, drawn twice for emphasis. Whoever Alder was, they had wanted to be followed. Wren traced the name once with a fingertip, then looked up toward the rising ground and the sure path Alder had marked.

choices.label: Head for high ground as Alder marked.

nodes.body: Wren stepped off the log onto the far bank just as the rookery erupted. Hundreds of herons lifted at once, a storm of grey wings and harsh cries that filled the whole sky. In the racket it was hard to think, harder still to see, and Wren nearly missed the path splitting ahead. One branch went left across a glistening mud slick, quick but treacherous. The other kept right, up through a stand of firm palms on solid footing. The birds wheeled and settled, wheeled again. Wren wiped a splash of mud from one cheek and looked at the two ways, knowing the wrong one could cost the whole morning.

choices.label: Cross the mud slick quickly.
Keep to the firm palms.

nodes.body: The mud slick was far deeper than it looked. One stride and it gripped Wren's boot like glue, then the other leg, sinking past the knee. Wren twisted and hauled and wriggled, breathing hard, and finally pulled free with a sucking pop, filthy to the thigh. But the struggle had cost precious minutes. Behind, the tide had quietly closed over the log bridge, and this whole finger of coast was cut off now, a corner Wren could not cross back into. It would stay a blank patch on the paper. Wren backed carefully to firmer ground and started the long way round, wiser and muddier, the day already smaller than it had been.

The ground lifted at last out of the swamp, mud giving way to firm palms and dry leaf litter underfoot. Wren climbed a low rise and stopped, breathing in the clean air. From here the whole northern shape of the island opened up, a long curl of reef offshore and, tucked into the cliffs, a bright notch of calm water that could only be a hidden cove. It was the first real view of the day, and it made the map feel possible. A goat track wound on ahead, one arm dropping toward that shining cove, another angling inland toward a green cleft where a spring gully seemed to run. Both led deeper into the island, and both looked worth the climb.

choices.label: Take the goat track down to the cove.
Angle inland toward a spring gully.

nodes.body: The oyster rocks were sharp underfoot and busy with crabs that scuttled sideways into every crack. Wren picked along them slowly, minding hands and boots on the cutting shells. Among the rocks lay a shape that did not belong. A small sea turtle had been tipped onto its back in a shrinking tide pool, flippers waving at the sky, stranded by the falling water. It was not hurt, only stuck, and time was against it as the pools drained toward the sea. Wren could stop and study the creature and read the way the tide moved through these pools, or search on among the rocks for a way off the shore and inland.

choices.label: Study the turtle and read the tide pools.
Search the rocks for a way inland.

nodes.body: Up close, the turtle was fine, just stuck, its dark eye rolling to watch Wren. Righting it now would be the kind thing, but the tide was dropping fast and every minute here was a minute stolen from the map. There was another way to help. Wren could mark the turtle's exact spot and sketch the reef channel that fed these pools, then send help back for it once a high vantage was found and the ship could be signalled. A quick rescue, or a careful note and a promise kept later. Wren looked from the waving flippers to the wide unmapped island and weighed the two against the falling sun.

choices.label: Mark the turtle and press on inland.
Stop and right the turtle now.

nodes.body: Wren knelt and tipped the turtle upright. It found its feet at once and scuttled down the wet rock into the sea, gone in a swirl of green. It was a good thing to have done, and Wren grinned to see it go. But the delay had let the tide flood back over the oyster rocks, and with wet paper and slipping light there was time to map only this one corner. Wren drew the reef honestly, every channel and rock set down true, a small square of coast done well. The rest of the island stayed blank behind it. Not the chart the mate wanted, perhaps, but an honest sketch and a kindness, and Wren could live with that.

Past the oyster rocks a thread of clear water crossed Wren's path, threading inland through the salt marsh. Wren cupped a handful and tasted it. Cold and sweet, growing colder and sweeter the higher it ran, which meant a spring somewhere above the swamp fed it. Fresh water always came from higher ground, and higher ground was exactly where a mapmaker needed to be. The seep made a natural guide, a silver line to follow up out of the mud and mangrove toward whatever rose beyond. Wren shouldered the satchel and set off along it, glad of a clear direction at last.

choices.label: Follow the clear seep upward.

nodes.body: The seep of clear water climbed up out of the marsh into a shaded gully of ferns, and the air turned cool and green. The water was properly cold now, tumbling over dark stones, and Wren stopped to fill the flask before going on. Ahead the gully forked. One arm climbed toward a bright gleam that could only be the spring pool itself, welling up somewhere higher. The other arm bent aside beneath a mossy overhang hung thick with vines, a shadowed place that looked as if it might shelter more than ferns. Both ways led up and away from the swamp. Wren listened to the trickle of the water and decided which fork to take.

choices.label: Climb to the bright spring pool.
Duck under the mossy overhang.

nodes.body: Wren ducked in under the mossy overhang, and the rock beneath was dry, out of the reach of rain and tide. Someone had sheltered here before. A ring of blackened stones marked an old firepit, and beside it lay a dented tin cup, long emptied. On the stone wall, scratched deep, was a single word, Alder, and below it an arrow pointing uphill toward what the marks named a camp. Wren ran a thumb over the letters. Whoever Alder was, they had passed this way and wanted to be followed. The shelter was snug and the day was long, but that arrow pulled at Wren, uphill, toward whatever waited at the camp.

choices.label: Follow Alder's arrow toward the camp.
Copy the firepit shelter and rest a while.

nodes.body: The dry shelter was so welcome after the swamp that Wren sat down to sketch it, the firepit, the cup, the scratched name. The pencil moved happily, and the morning slid by unfelt, quiet under the mossy roof. When the light finally shifted across the wall, Wren looked up with a start. Only this one snug corner was drawn, careful and small, while the whole island lay bare beyond it. Wren scrambled up, brushed off the dust, and hurried out into the gully, knowing the sun was higher than it had any right to be and far too much paper was still white.

The hidden creek ran inland straight and slow beneath a green roof of leaves, so quiet that Wren could hear the water lapping the roots. It made easy, level going after the swamp. Around a bend came a soft splashing, and there they were, a mother otter and two round kits watching from a half-sunk log. For a moment nobody moved. Then the otters slipped off the log and vanished upstream in a line of silver bubbles. Wren smiled and considered the ways on. The otters had gone up the creek, firm trees rose off to one side, and across the water a loud heron island crowded the channel with noise and wings.

choices.label: Follow the otters upstream.
Cut away from the water into the trees.
Pole across to the loud heron island.

nodes.body: Upstream the creek shallowed over a clean gravel bar, clear water running bright over the stones, and otters played in the riffles below. Beyond the bar the ground rose firm and dry, out of the reach of the tide at last. Wren cupped a drink from the creek. It was sweet, not salt, fed from somewhere higher up the island where the real land began. Fresh water climbing meant a spring above, and a spring meant high ground, and high ground meant a view. Wren stepped up onto the dry bank and followed the sweet water on, upward, glad to leave the mud behind.

choices.label: Climb along the sweet water.

nodes.body: Away from the creek the trees closed in, and the light went green and dim beneath them. Without the water to follow, Wren had to choose a heading by feel alone, which was exactly the sort of thing that lost people in a mangrove. Off to one side, uphill, a faint brightness showed through the trunks, the promise of open ground and a view. The other way, a level game trail ran off flat and easy through the undergrowth, tempting because it asked so little. Wren stood still and listened. Easy was not always right, and bright was not always near. One of these ways would lead out, and one might only lead deeper.

choices.label: Climb toward the brightness.
Take the level game trail.

nodes.body: The game trail ran level and easy, just as it had promised, and for a while Wren made good time along it. But it kept curling back on itself, bending left, then left again, until with a cold jolt Wren realized the trouble. There was no true landmark here, no rise, no shore, only more mangrove in every direction, all of it looking exactly the same. The trail could be a loop, and a loop went nowhere. Behind and above, that patch of brightness still showed faint through the leaves, the one thing that was not more of the same. Wren could turn back toward it now, or gamble on one more bend and hope the trail broke out.

choices.label: Backtrack toward the bright rise.
Gamble on one more bend.

nodes.body: Wren took the gamble and pushed on around one more bend. The trail gave onto a fallen tree, half rotted, unmistakable, the very same tree Wren had passed an hour before. The mangrove had been walking Wren in a slow, patient circle the whole time. The sun stood high now, and there was no undoing the wasted hour. Wren drew the coast as best it could be guessed, an honest rough shape with the middle left frankly blank, because guessing a map you cannot see is no map at all. Then Wren turned back toward firmer ground, ready to start again where the land could actually be seen.

The cloud-forest ridge climbed straight up off the beach into cool grey mist. Moss dripped from every branch, and birdsong fell from somewhere overhead, unseen in the fog. It was a green, dripping world, and it smelled of wet earth and growing things. Wren started up and soon found the ways parting. A well-worn switchback climbed steeply, zigzagging up the face of the slope for anyone with strong legs. A gentler traverse angled across the hillside, longer but kinder. A side path dropped away to a mossy hollow where something pale gleamed in the shadows. And through the trees came a low roar, the sound of falling water, pulling toward a hidden fall. Wren wiped mist from both eyes and chose a way up.

choices.label: Take the steep switchback up.
Angle across the gentle traverse.
Drop to the pale gleam in the hollow.
Follow the roar toward a hidden waterfall.

nodes.body: The switchback was steep but honest, no tricks in it, only hard climbing that paid off with every turn. Each bend opened a wider view of the island falling away below, the coast and the reef and the far pale flats. Wren stopped to breathe on a flat ledge where the path went on two ways. Straight up rose a bare rock rib, exposed and airy, a quick hard line for anyone bold enough to take it. Curving off to the side ran a green shelf, level and sheltered, tracing toward a break in the trees. Both climbed toward the ridge above. Wren caught a breath, checked the footing, and picked the way that suited the legs and the nerve.

choices.label: Climb the bare rock rib.
Follow the green shelf.

nodes.body: The rock rib was airy and exposed, cloud streaming past on either side, and Wren went up it with care, testing each hold before trusting it with weight. It was frightening in the best way, all height and open air, and it gained ground fast. Hand over hand, boot after boot, Wren climbed until the rib delivered onto a broad grassy bench where the true ridge began. Here the wind combed the tussocks and the world spread out below, and a worn path ran on toward higher ground, the sure sign of feet that had passed this way many times before. Wren stood tall, heart still pounding, and followed it on.

choices.label: Follow the ridge path higher.

nodes.body: The green shelf traced a level line across the slope, and the more Wren walked it the plainer its purpose became. It held its height exactly, curving patiently around every fold of the hill, neither climbing nor dropping. This was a contour path, the very sort a surveyor cuts to move sideways without losing hard-won height. Someone had made it on purpose, long ago, and used it often. Wren followed it around the shoulder of the slope, glad of the easy footing after the climb, and it brought them out, sure enough, onto the same grassy bench where the true ridge path began its long climb toward the high ground.

choices.label: Join the ridge path.

nodes.body: The grassy bench was the ridge's broad shoulder, and up here the wind was a live thing, combing the tussocks flat and tugging at Wren's satchel. The mist had thinned, and the whole spine of the island lifted ahead. A stony trail climbed on from here toward the high camp Wren kept hearing about, the place every clue seemed to point to. Off to one side, though, stood something else, a hush of giant mossy trees marking an ancient grove, older and stiller than the rest of the forest. The trail called upward, plain and purposeful. The grove called sideways, quiet and strange. Wren stood on the windy bench and weighed the pull of each.

choices.label: Climb the stony trail toward the camp.
Detour into the grove of ancient trees.

nodes.body: The last stretch toward the camp was a scramble over broken rock, where the path all but vanished among the tumbled stones. It would have been easy to lose the way here and waste an hour casting about. But someone careful had come before. A line of small cairns marched up the slope ahead, each little pile of balanced stones set by a steady hand to point the safe way through the rubble. Wren followed them from one to the next, trusting the pattern, hands finding holds where the cairns said to look. They led surely upward, out of the broken ground and on toward the high ridge where the camp had to stand.

choices.label: Follow the cairns onto the high ridge.

nodes.body: Up on the open ridge the wind was a wall, and the world fell away on both sides into cloud and distance. It was thrilling and a little terrifying at once, walking the top of the island with the whole sky for company. The cairns marched on ahead, small grey markers stepping off toward a far speck that had to be the camp itself. The ground rose in long tired steps of rock and heather, up and up, each rise revealing another beyond it. Wren tightened the satchel strap, leaned into the wind, and kept to the line of cairns, one careful stone at a time toward that distant grey shape.

choices.label: Push on along the cairned ridge.

nodes.body: The ridge narrowed and split around a shoulder of bare rock, and Wren stopped to read the ground. The cairns went left, tracing a sheltered ledge that curled below the crest, out of the worst of the wind and safely back from the drop. A bolder line went right, straight over the airy crest itself, where the whole island lay spread on both sides and the wind pushed hard at your back. One way was patient and sure. The other was quicker and grander and far more exposed. And a third feeling rose too, the plain urge to stop here, daunted by the height, and go no further at all.

choices.label: Take the sheltered ledge.
Brave the airy crest.
Stop here, daunted by the height.

nodes.body: The drop fell away on both sides, sheer and dizzying, and Wren's head began to swim just looking at it. The wind seemed to lean in and pull. For a long frozen minute Wren could not make either foot move forward, and the only honest thing left was to sit down, breathe, and edge slowly back the way that had felt safe. That was not cowardice, whatever it felt like. A mapmaker who freezes on a high ridge and pushes on anyway is a mapmaker who falls. Wren mapped the ridge carefully from lower down, and left the high camp a grey speck out of reach, its chart saved for a braver day.

Wren took the sheltered ledge, and it was slow but sure, tucked below the crest out of the worst of the wind. The going was patient work, one steady step after another, with a hand always resting on the rock at shoulder height. Cushions of tiny alpine flowers grew in the cracks, pink and white, clinging where almost nothing else could. Wren passed them carefully, minding each foothold, and the ledge held true. Little by little it climbed, threading below the ridgeline, until at last it lifted back up to the crest where the two ways came together again. Wren stepped up onto the ridge, glad of the safe road taken.

choices.label: Rejoin the ridgeline.

nodes.body: Wren braved the airy crest, and it was pure exhilaration from the first step. This was the very spine of the island, a narrow walk with the whole world dropping away on both hands and cloud pouring silently over from the far side like a slow white river. Wren went carefully, arms out, eyes on the footing, but grinning all the while, because there was nowhere higher or wilder to be. The wind pushed and the cloud streamed and the crest ran on, and then it eased and broadened, and Wren came down breathless and beaming to a level shelf where the cairns gathered again, thick and reassuring on the flat ground.

choices.label: Come down to the level shelf.

nodes.body: Here the two ways came back together on a broad level shelf, where the last cairns clustered thick, as if many travellers had paused in this same spot to gather themselves. The ground was flat and blessedly wide after the narrow climbing, and the wind dropped for a moment in the lee of a low rise. Wren stood among the little stone piles and looked up. One short pull remained, over grass and rock, toward the cliff where the camp had to stand at last. It was close now, close enough to feel. Wren shouldered the satchel, checked the sketchbook was dry and safe, and made ready for the final climb.

choices.label: Make the final pull to the camp.

nodes.body: And there, at last, it was. Tucked against the base of the cliff, sheltered from the worst of the wind, stood the grey canvas roof and low stone walls of a camp, small and weathered and unmistakable. Alder's camp, exactly where every clue on the island had been quietly pointing all day long, the driftwood arrows and the scratched names and the line of patient cairns. Wren stopped and simply looked at it for a moment, hardly believing the climb was done. Then, heart lifting, Wren crossed the last stretch of grass and rock toward the low doorway and stepped up to the threshold of the place.

choices.label: Step into Alder's camp.

nodes.body: The traverse was gentle but long, angling across the wooded slope, and it crossed one small stream after another, every one of them running the same way, downhill toward the sea. Wren read that pattern the way a mapmaker learns to. Follow water up and it leads to its source. So Wren traced one stream upward against its flow, and it opened into a clear cold pool ringed with ferns, welling up cool and quiet from the rock. This, plainly, was the island's spring, the place where all that water began. Wren could rest here at the heart of it, keep on across the slope, or follow a narrow deer path aside toward a hidden dell.

choices.label: Rest at the spring.
Keep traversing along the slope.
Follow a deer path to a hidden dell.

nodes.body: Higher up the slope the mist thinned to rags, and a stand of wind-bent trees gave way to open ground where the light came clean and grey. Wren paused to get a bearing. A faint path climbed on from here, up toward the ridge and the high ground where the real views waited. And below it, off to one side, a mossy hollow showed the same pale gleam that had winked up from the beach that morning, half hidden among the ferns and rock. Both pulled at Wren. Up meant height and progress and the camp beyond. Down meant the pale mystery, whatever it was, waiting quietly in the shadow of the hollow.

choices.label: Climb the faint path on.
Drop to the pale gleam.

nodes.body: Wren dropped down toward the pale gleam, and it turned out to be a curtain of white orchids spilling over a rock face, hundreds of small blooms glowing in the green shade. They were beautiful enough to stop the breath. But behind them, when Wren parted the flowers gently, there was more. A dry cleft ran back into the rock, and wedged into it sat a tin box, green with age and furred with moss, its lid marked with a small compass rose. Someone had hidden it here on purpose and trusted the orchids to keep it. Wren's fingers hovered over the lid. Open it, or leave it and climb back up to the higher ground.

choices.label: Open the tin box.
Leave the box and climb back up.

nodes.body: The tin box came free with a soft grating sound, and Wren eased the lid up. Inside, kept dry all these years, lay folded papers, a stub of pencil worn to the wood, and a small brass locket gone dark with age. Wren unfolded the top paper, and it was field notes, written in a careful, practiced hand, columns of bearings and little sketched shapes. The locket, when it clicked open, held a faded picture of a girl, young, half-smiling, with something in the eyes that looked a little like Wren's own face. Two mysteries in one box. Wren could sit and read the notes through, or pocket the locket and hurry on with the puzzle unsolved.

choices.label: Read the field notes.
Pocket the locket and hurry on.

nodes.body: Wren slipped the little brass locket into a pocket and climbed on, but the girl in the faded picture would not leave. Who was she? Why left here, in a tin, behind a curtain of orchids? Wren spent the rest of the morning turning it over and over, eyes on the ground, mind far away, and the ridge got mapped in a distracted, wandering scrawl that would shame a first-year student. It was not the day's work the mate had asked for. But some questions weigh more than a finished chart, and Wren carried the small mystery home instead, the locket warm in one pocket and the map barely begun.

Wren read the field notes through, and the careful hand had a name at the bottom, Alder, the mapmaker who had come to this island before. The notes told a plain, sad, hopeful story. Alder had drawn a full chart of the island and hidden it safe at a camp up on the ridge, meaning to come back for it. There was a warning too, underlined twice, that the caverns below flooded fast and deep at high tide, and no one should be caught in them. The last line pointed simply uphill, toward the camp and the finished map. Wren folded the notes away, took heart from them, and turned to follow Alder's trail up toward the ridge.

choices.label: Follow Alder's notes toward the camp.

nodes.body: A cool breath of air rose out of the sink-hole, ringed with dripping ferns, and Wren felt it on both cheeks like the island exhaling. Someone had been here before, and recently. A knotted rope hung down into the dark, tied off to a sturdy root at the rim. Wren tested the anchor with a hard pull, checked the knots, and climbed down hand over hand into the cool below. The shaft opened into a cavern where three passages led away. From one came the steady echo of dripping water. Another glittered faintly, catching what little light fell. The third breathed warm air up from somewhere deeper in. And high in the roof, a hole of pale daylight promised a way back up. Wren let go of the rope and chose.

choices.label: Follow the dripping water.
Head toward the glitter.
Trace the warm breathing air.
Climb toward a high hole of daylight.

nodes.body: The dripping passage sloped gently down to an underground stream, clear and cold and running fast toward a low arch in the rock. Wren crouched to drink and read the walls the way a careful explorer must. High on one side ran a scratched line, deliberate, marking exactly where the water rose at the turn of the tide, a warning left by whoever came before. Wren noted it well and stayed below it. From here the stream offered three ways. Wade down with the current toward the low arch. Climb up against the flow toward wherever the water was born. Or slip aside into a narrow rift where glowworms lit the dark with a faint blue glow.

choices.label: Wade the stream toward the arch.
Climb the stream upward against the flow.
Explore a side rift lit with glowworms.

nodes.body: The arch was low, and the water beneath it was rising, sliding up the rock in slow cold pulses. Wren stopped and thought hard, because a rising tide in a cave is nothing to gamble with. Beside the arch a dry ledge ran on above the water, curving toward a paler light somewhere ahead, safe footing that stayed clear of the flood. But under the arch itself the passage bored straight into the dark heart of the caves, a shortcut, if it could be crossed before the water closed it. Wren watched the water climb another inch up the stone. The high dry ledge, or the low dark arch, and only one of them respected the tide.

choices.label: Edge along the dry ledge to the light.
Duck under the low arch.

nodes.body: Wren ducked under the low arch, and past it the roof dropped until it hung barely a hand's width above the water. For a heartbeat Wren hesitated, judging the gap, and in that heartbeat the tide pushed in. The passage began to fill, cold water climbing the walls fast, exactly as the scratched line had warned. There was just time to scramble back, gasping, soaked to the chest, hauling out under the arch as the water rose behind. Wren stood shaking on the dry ledge, heart slamming, and let the fright teach its lesson. The caverns kept their heart to themselves. On the paper they became a single dark question mark, and Wren was glad to be alive to draw even that.

The way to the spring ran upstream, against the cold current, and Wren climbed it steadily, boots braced on the streambed. The higher the climb, the sweeter the water tasted, and the more the roof lifted overhead. At last the passage opened into a bright chamber where a shaft of daylight fell straight down from a crack far above, lighting a still pool at the bottom. Wren stood in that shaft of light and understood. This was the underground face of the island's spring, the place where the fresh water first welled up out of the rock before it ever saw the sky. The daylight climbed away above, toward the surface and the world of green and wind.

choices.label: Climb the daylight shaft to the spring.

nodes.body: The glitter was not treasure, and Wren felt the small tug of disappointment before the wonder took over. It was crystal, veins of clear quartz webbing the cavern walls, catching the faint light and throwing it back in a thousand tiny sparks. But among the crystal was something far older and stranger. Handprints, pressed in red ochre onto the smooth stone, faded but plain, whole and open, a scatter of them across the wall. Someone had marked this place long before any mapmaker came, before charts existed at all, reaching out to touch the rock with a painted palm. Wren stared, moved. Copy the handprints carefully, or press on deeper toward a further glow in the dark.

choices.label: Copy the handprints carefully.
Push past toward a further glow.

nodes.body: Wren began copying the handprints, and as the pencil traced them a pattern came clear that raised the hair on both arms. The prints were not scattered at random. Together they made a rough shape, and the shape was the island itself, drawn by pressing living hands against the stone. There was a mark where the spring rose, and another at the high peak, the two hearts of the place. It was a map, the oldest map of all, made by the first people to find this island, long before the Chart House or Alder or anyone. Wren could follow the ochre mark toward the spring, or fill a whole page with the ancient painting exactly as it was.

choices.label: Follow the ochre mark toward the spring.
Fill a whole page with the old painting.

nodes.body: Wren filled a whole page with the ochre handprints and the shape of the island painted around them, every print, every faded line, copied true. It was a record no chart had ever held, proof in red earth that people had lived and mapped here long before any surveyor set foot on the beach. The Chart House would never have seen its like. It cost the afternoon, and the land above stayed half drawn, its coasts and ridges waiting. But when the keepers gathered around Wren's page and understood what it showed, the hush in the room said the trade had been a fair one after all. Some maps are older than maps, and worth the whole day.

Wren pushed toward the glow, and it grew from a thread to a crack to a bright seam of daylight leaking through the rock. Squeezing up toward it, boots scrabbling, Wren tumbled out blinking into open air onto a hidden shelf of beach ringed all around by high cliffs. It was the hidden cove, seen from below now, its calm water lapping quiet inside the reef. Wren stood in the sudden brightness, half laughing with relief. Back the way the passage had come, a side branch murmured with the sound of lapping water, deeper and darker. Ahead lay the cove itself, and a way up. Wren looked from the light to the dark and chose.

choices.label: Climb up into the cove.
Follow the passage toward the lapping water.

nodes.body: The warm passage breathed in and out like a sleeping animal, and around a bend Wren found the reason for it. A colony of little bats hung from the roof, packed close and softly stirring, and the warmth was the warmth of all their small bodies together. But there was something else on a low ledge below them. A young seal pup lay curled there, far from any sea, its dark eyes catching the dim light. It had wriggled in through some flooded entrance from the ocean and now could not climb back out, stranded in the warm dark. Wren crouched slowly, not wanting to frighten it. Study how the pup had come in, or back quietly away and leave the warm dark to itself.

choices.label: Study how the pup got in.
Back away and leave the warm dark.

nodes.body: Wren studied the pup and the ledge and worked out the story. It had come through a flooded tube from the sea, then been left stranded when the low tide drained the water away, too high now to climb back down. It was frightened but unhurt. Wren could not lift it alone, that much was plain, a seal pup is heavier and stronger than it looks. But there was another way to help. Mark the exact spot on the map, climb to a high place, and signal the ship, and grown hands might come before dusk to carry it out. A careful plan and a promise, or a lone, likely hopeless try at coaxing it free right now.

choices.label: Mark the pup and climb toward the light.
Try to coax the pup out alone now.

nodes.body: Wren tried to coax the pup out alone, and it was hopeless from the start. The pup was heavier than it looked and frightened besides, twisting and barking, and the wet rock gave no grip. After a long slippery struggle that got them both nowhere, Wren had to sit back, breathing hard, and admit the truth. This needed more than one pair of hands. Wren marked the sea tube carefully on the map, so help could find it, and backed away, leaving the pup where it lay. The rescue would have to wait for the rising tide and better luck than a lone apprentice could give. It was hard to walk away, but the wrong kind of trying only makes things worse.

Wren backed slowly out of the warm dark, careful not to startle the sleeping bats, and felt along the passage for another way. Off to one side a side vent opened, choked with dangling roots, and beyond the roots showed a thin slot of daylight. Wren pushed the roots aside and peered through. The slot opened onto firm ground high on the slope, out in the clean air and the light, well above the reach of any tide. That was the way up and out. Wren wriggled toward it, glad of solid rock and open sky ahead after the breathing dark of the bat cave, and squeezed toward the daylight.

choices.label: Squeeze out toward the daylight.

nodes.body: Wren climbed toward the light and air, up where a crack in the rock opened on the day, and a few late bats flickered out past into the brightness ahead. The passage steepened and then gave way, and Wren came up out of the ground onto the open flank of the ridge, blinking in the sudden wind. The whole island tilted away below, coast and reef and pale flats spread out under the racing cloud, and the sight of it after the dark caves was pure gladness. Better still, not far above, the grey canvas of the ridge camp showed against the higher rock. Wren brushed off the cave dust and set off up the open slope toward it.

choices.label: Make for the ridge camp.

nodes.body: The survey tower leaned on its headland, half its stone stair fallen away, a place built by mapmakers before Wren was even born. Wren walked up to it with a kind of awe. This was where the craft had lived once, and the sea wind sang through its broken windows now. The door hung open on a ground room full of shadow. Outside, a spiral stair climbed the tower's flank toward the broken top, missing steps but climbable for a careful foot. And set into the floor near the threshold was a cellar hatch, its iron ring furred with rust. There was a keeper's quarters too, a low door off to one side. Wren stood in the old doorway and decided where to begin.

choices.label: Search the ground room.
Climb the outside stair.
Lift the cellar hatch.
Explore the keeper's side quarters.

nodes.body: The ground room was full of ruined instruments, and Wren moved through them like a visitor in a tomb. A rusted theodolite stood on its three legs, seized solid. Rotten ropes hung in loops from pegs, and cracked lenses lay scattered in the dust. In the middle stood a slate desk, and on it a logbook, swollen fat with years of damp, its cover warped. Wren eased it open. The last pages were stained but still just readable, a fading hand recording the final days of whoever had kept this tower. There was a story in there, half drowned. Wren could read the swollen logbook, salvage a working instrument from the wreckage, or pry up a loose slab that shifted underfoot in the floor.

choices.label: Read the swollen logbook.
Salvage a working instrument.
Pry up a loose slab in the floor.

nodes.body: The logbook was Alder's, and Wren read the last entry twice to be sure of it. The true chart of the island, it said, had been carried up to the ridge camp for safekeeping before a great storm, and Alder had meant to return for it. The words simply stopped after that. Wren copied them carefully into the sketchbook, the whole sad, hopeful record, but the reading had eaten the light, and the pencil-hand was slower than it should have been. When Wren finally looked up, dusk was leaning close. Home went a story of the lost mapmaker, faithfully copied, in place of a finished map. Some days a mapmaker carries back a life instead of a coastline.

Wren searched the ruined instruments for anything that still worked, and found one. An alidade, a battered brass sighting rule for fixing distant points, its edge still true, its little sights still swinging free. Wren turned it in both hands, hardly believing the luck. With this, from any high place, the island's whole shape could be fixed fast and sure, bearing by bearing, the way a real surveyor works. But a tool is only worth the height you carry it to. Wren could take the alidade straight inland toward the ridge camp and the high ground beyond, or carry it up the tower's own broken stair first, to try the view from the top right here.

choices.label: Take the alidade toward the ridge camp.
Take it up the tower stair first.

nodes.body: With the tower's find gathered up and stowed safe, Wren set off inland along the headland path. It ran firm and clear above the rocks, climbing steadily to meet the island's main ridge where the two heights joined. The sea fell away behind, and the wind grew stronger and cleaner with every step upward. Far ahead, small against the grey cliff, the canvas of the ridge camp showed at last, the place all the day's clues kept pointing toward. Wren fixed the eyes on it and kept climbing, glad to be leaving the ruined tower for the living ridge, carrying whatever the old place had given up toward the high ground where it could be used.

choices.label: Follow the headland path to the ridge.

nodes.body: The outside stair spiraled up the tower's flank, missing steps here and there, open on one side to a long drop to the rocks below. Wren went up slowly, keeping close to the wall, one hand always on the stone, testing each step before trusting it. Two thirds of the way up, a whole span of stair had fallen, leaving a gap. Beyond it the next slab waited, close enough to reach with a bold stride if the nerve held. To one side, a crumbling ledge offered a slower way around, clinging to the wall. Wren stood at the gap, wind tugging, and judged the reach against the fall, because up here a wrong choice was not a small one.

choices.label: Stride boldly across the gap.
Clamber the crumbling ledge.

nodes.body: Wren edged out onto the crumbling ledge, and it began to shed stones under both boots at once, small pieces skittering off into the long drop below. That was the whole answer, right there. There was nothing to do but retreat, slowly, hands flat on the wall, heart pounding, back to the solid part of the stair. A ledge that crumbles when you test it is a ledge that says no, and a wise climber listens. Wren climbed carefully back down to the ground, legs shaking with the leftover fear. The tower kept its high view to itself. That corner of the island got drawn from guesswork below, honest but incomplete, and Wren was fine with the trade.

From the tower top the island showed its bones, and Wren turned slowly, drinking it in. The long spine of the ridge, the pale curve of the flats, the white line of the reef, and there, tucked in its ring of cliffs, the bright still water of a hidden cove. A surveyor could take rough bearings from up here and get the shape half right. But the true summit stood higher still, away across the ridge, reached only by way of the camp, and a rough shape was not a finished chart. Three ways led on. Cross the ridge toward the camp. Climb down toward that hidden cove. Or examine the old beacon brazier standing rusted on the headland's point.

choices.label: Cross the ridge toward the camp.
Climb down toward the hidden cove.
Examine the old beacon brazier.

nodes.body: The way from the tower to the main ridge ran along a knife of rock, the sea falling away on both sides, thrilling and slow. Wren walked it with care, eyes on the footing, keeping low where the wind gusted hardest, never hurrying. It was no place to be careless, and no place to be timid either, only steady. The rock underfoot was sound, and the view on either hand was enormous, the whole blue world tilting away below. Little by little the knife broadened and softened, until it joined the wide grassy bench where the camp trail began its climb. Wren stepped off the narrow rock onto solid ground with a long breath of relief and turned uphill.

choices.label: Join the camp trail.

nodes.body: The cellar was dry and cool, and Wren climbed down into it to find the surveyors' old stores still waiting in the dark. Coils of rope hung from pegs, sound enough to trust. Tins of oil and food stood ranked on a shelf, and a lamp sat ready by the stair. This was a place stocked for long careful work, everything a mapmaker might need. And rolled against the far wall, tied with a faded ribbon, stood a leather tube of the kind used to carry charts safely. Wren's heart quickened. It might hold the very map the whole island seemed to be whispering about. Open the leather tube, or take rope and oil and climb back out ready for cliff and cave.

choices.label: Open the leather tube.
Take rope and oil and climb back out.

nodes.body: The leather tube held a chart, and Wren unrolled it with careful hands, hardly breathing. But it was half finished and badly water-stained, Alder's abandoned draft, the coast begun and the high ground left blank. A note in the corner explained it plainly. The good copy, it said, had been carried to the ridge camp, and this rough one left behind. Still, it showed enough. The safe path up toward the ridge was sketched clear, a gift from the mapmaker who came first. Wren could follow the draft's guidance inland toward the camp, or sit down and copy the whole ruined thing in full, every surviving line of Alder's unfinished island.

choices.label: Follow the draft toward the ridge camp.
Sit and copy the ruined draft in full.

nodes.body: Wren sat in the cool cellar and copied Alder's ruined draft, reading each water-stained line and setting it down afresh, and it was real work, careful and true. But when it was done, Wren looked at the page and felt the flaw in it. This was Alder's map, not Wren's. Every line was traced from someone else's seeing, and the blank half was still blank because Alder had never lived to fill it. The day ended with a faithful copy of another mapmaker's unfinished island and no new ground of Wren's own upon it. A copy is a kind of honoring, Wren thought, climbing out at dusk, but it is not the same as looking with your own eyes.

Wren coiled a length of good rope over one shoulder and stowed a tin of oil, ready now for cliff or cave or whatever the island still hid. A prepared mapmaker fears fewer places. And there, chalked faintly on the cellar wall by the door, was a surveyor's line, a straight guide mark pointing out and away, along a marked path toward the high ground of the ridge. Someone had left it to speed the next person on. Wren followed the chalk line up out of the cellar and into the light, rope swinging, and set off along the marked way inland, glad to be moving toward the ridge with the right gear on both shoulders.

choices.label: Follow the chalk line to the ridge.

nodes.body: The tidal flats stretched wide and mirror-bright, and Wren stepped out onto them warily. Near shore the sand was firm underfoot, but farther out it turned to soft grey ooze where the sea had drawn far back. Everyone at the Chart House knew the rule of such places. The water would return, and it would come in faster than a person could run. So a mapmaker on the flats keeps one eye always on the sea. A distant sandbar showed far out, crowded with birds and pale with something wrecked. Firm channel edges threaded across the mud like roads. The strand line near shore glittered with cast-up treasures. And dark mussel beds crunched off to one side. Wren checked the far water, then chose a way out.

choices.label: Wade toward the distant sandbar.
Trace the firm channel edges.
Search the strand line near shore.
Pick across the dark mussel beds.

nodes.body: The sandbar was a treasure for a mapmaker, littered with old wreck timber and, half buried, the whole ribbed hull of a boat gone to bones. It would draw beautifully. But Wren felt the change underfoot before seeing it, the sand darkening as water crept up from below, and out at the edge of sight the sea had already turned and begun its long slide back in. That was the warning. There were minutes here, not hours, and the flats were no place to misjudge them. Wren could sketch fast and hard and then run for shore while the running was still good, or linger over the wreck a little too long and trust the tide to be slow. It would not be.

choices.label: Sketch fast, then run for shore.
Linger over the wreck too long.

nodes.body: The sketch was good, careful and true, and the run was simply not fast enough. Out here the sea fills the low channels first, cutting a person off from shore before the flats themselves are even wet, and that was exactly what happened. Wren waded back chest-deep through a channel that had been dry sand minutes before, sketchbook held high overhead in both hands, heart pounding, feet feeling for the bottom. Wren made it, soaked and shaking, and that was the thing that mattered. But the rest of the day went to drying out on the shore, and the flats stayed a bright blank on the paper, a lesson written in cold seawater about the speed of a turning tide.

Wren sketched the wreck in furious strokes, eyes flicking between the pencil and the creeping water, and the moment the drawing was true enough, ran. Boots pounding the darkening sand, satchel bouncing, Wren reached firm ground just as the first thin sheet of water slid hissing across the flats behind, filling the way back. That was how it was done, fast and decided, respecting the sea's speed instead of testing it. Wren stood on the safe sand, chest heaving, grinning at the near thing of it. The flats were drawn and the feet were dry, both. Ahead, a green gap opened in the shoreline where the land climbed inland, a dry way on, and Wren took it.

choices.label: Follow the green gap inland.

nodes.body: The green gap was the mouth of a valley, where a stream came tumbling down to meet the sea between banks of grass and reed. Wren followed it up on the firm dry banks, glad of solid ground under both boots after the flats. The stream led inland and slightly up, and as Wren climbed, the air grew cooler and a sound came drifting down to meet the water, the steady rush of falling water somewhere ahead. Somewhere up this valley the stream was born, at a fall or a spring, the high heart of the island's water. Wren kept to the firm bank and climbed on toward the sound, leaving the mirror-flats shining behind and below.

choices.label: Climb the stream toward the falls.

nodes.body: The firm channel edges made a natural road across the flats, packed and sure underfoot where the soft mud sucked on either side, and Wren followed them the way a sensible traveller follows a causeway. Keep to the firm line, and the crossing stayed safe. The channels ran together as they neared the shore, gathering at the foot of a ring of high cliffs, where a gap of dark wet sand led inward, bared only by the low tide. Three ways offered themselves. Follow the channels on to that cliff gap and whatever lay beyond it. Cut inland up a firm rise toward the higher ground. Or detour out to the great wreck stranded on the bar. Wren judged the water and chose.

choices.label: Follow the channels to the cliff gap.
Cut inland up a firm rise.
Detour to the great wreck on the bar.

nodes.body: The cliff gap was a slot of dark wet sand, and Wren understood at once that the sea only left it bare at low water, that in a few hours it would be deep under the returning tide. It was a door with a closing time. Wren stepped through quickly, boots sinking a little in the wet sand, into the shelter of the rock. And there, opening quiet and calm beyond the gap, lay a hidden pocket of beach ringed all around by cliffs, a secret cove that no chart had ever shown. The water inside it lay still as a held breath. Wren stood in the gap a moment, taking in the calm of the place, then went on into the cove.

choices.label: Explore the hidden cove.

nodes.body: The strand line was a museum of the sea, and Wren walked it slowly, reading the cast-up treasures. Shells lay heaped in drifts. A tangle of old rope, silvered by salt. A green glass net float, whole and gleaming, rolled up out of some fisherman's loss. And half buried in the wrack, catching the light, a corked bottle with a curl of pale paper visible inside it. That stopped Wren's feet. A message, sealed and cast up here on this nameless shore. It could wait, or it could not. Wren looked from the bottle to the sweep of the beach. Open the bottle and read what it held, leave it and read the shoreline instead, or climb the grassy dunes behind for a better view.

choices.label: Open the bottle.
Leave it and read the shoreline.
Climb the grassy dunes behind the beach.

nodes.body: The cork came free with a squeak, and Wren teased out the curl of paper with two careful fingers. It was a message, and the hand was one Wren was coming to know. A mapmaker named Alder had written it, plain and warm. This island is real, it said, and worth the naming, and the full chart of it waits at the ridge camp for whoever finishes the work. It was an invitation across the years, sealed in glass and given to the sea in hope. Wren read it twice, the wind snapping the little paper. Set out inland now for the ridge camp and take up the work, or keep the note as a fine find and stop here, satisfied.

choices.label: Set out inland for the ridge camp.
Keep the note as a find and stop here.

nodes.body: Wren tucked Alder's message away safe and turned back toward the beach to wait for the ship, carrying a mystery home in place of a map. It was a good story, the bottle, the invitation, the mapmaker calling across the years, and Wren would tell it well. But a blank chart is still a blank chart, however fine the tale that comes with it, and the mate had asked for lines on paper, not legends. The apprenticeship would have to wait for another island and a steadier day. Wren sat on the warm sand as the light lengthened, the little note safe in a pocket, and watched the sea for the returning sail without much regret.

The hidden cove was a perfect pocket of calm inside the reef, and Wren saw at once why a mapmaker would love it. The water lay still and clear as green glass, sheltered from every wind, a place no chart had ever shown to the world. It felt like a secret kept just for whoever was lucky enough to find it. A thread of fresh water trickled down one rock wall, sweet and cold. A path cut long ago by hand climbed away up toward the ridge. And on a flat rock near the water, someone had drawn an old compass rose, its arrow pointing plainly up. Wren stood in the hush of the place. Map the cove and climb the rill toward the spring, follow the compass rose up toward the ridge, search the tide pools and the sea cave, or simply rest a while in the calm.

choices.label: Map the cove, then climb the rill toward the spring.
Follow the compass rose up toward the ridge.
Search the cove's tide pools and sea cave.
Rest too long in the calm cove.

nodes.body: The cove was so calm that Wren sat down on a warm rock to sketch it, and the stillness did its quiet work. The pencil slowed, the eyes wandered, and the morning slipped away almost unnoticed, one lovely finished corner taking shape on the paper while the shadows crept round unseen. When at last a change in the light made Wren startle and look up, the truth was plain and unwelcome. Only the cove was drawn, one perfect little pocket of coast in a whole blank island. Wren scrambled up, gathered the satchel, and hurried off toward higher ground, knowing dusk was closer now than it had any right to be.

The hand-cut path climbed steeply away from the low ground, switchbacking up the rock past the reach of the sea, and Wren followed it, glad of the old handholds and cut steps worn by feet long gone. Someone had made this way on purpose, and made it well. Halfway up, where the path leveled for a breath, a great slab of rock tilted toward the sky, and its whole face was pecked with small round dots. Wren stopped. The dots were not random, that much was plain even at a glance, they had a pattern, a scatter with meaning in it. Study the dotted slab and read what it held, or press straight on up toward the ridge and the high ground waiting above.

choices.label: Study the dotted slab.
Press straight on up to the ridge.

nodes.body: Wren crouched over the tilted slab and read the dots, and understanding came like a cool wind. It was a star map. This was how the island's first people had found their way here across the dark water, by night, steering under these very stars. The pecked dots marked the guiding stars themselves, and among them a single deeper scratch marked the island's high peak beneath them, the mark to steer toward. It was navigation older than any chart, older than the Chart House, a whole way of finding the world written in stone. It was a wonder worth a whole page. Copy it quickly and climb on toward the ridge, or copy it in full and go no further.

choices.label: Copy it quickly and climb on.
Copy it in full and go no further.

nodes.body: Wren filled a page with the star slab, every guiding dot and the deep-scratched peak beneath them, and the shape of the island drawn around it as the first people must have seen it from the sea. It was the oldest map of all, drawn in stars. But when the last dot was down, the light had turned, and the long climb to the ridge was simply too far now to reach in time. The chart of the coast stayed unfinished. Yet what Wren carried home instead was no small thing, and when the Chart House keepers gathered round the page and understood it, they fell quiet and then leaned close, the way people do before something truly old and true.

The cut path topped out at last on the broad grassy bench of the main ridge, and the change was sudden and total. Wind and cloud and open sky, all at once, after the still green hush of the low ground behind. Wren stood in the buffeting air, hair whipping, and felt the whole island rise around and above. From here the way was plain. The stony camp trail lifted away up the ridge toward the grey canvas of the shelter that every clue on the island had been pointing to all day. Wren pulled the satchel strap tight against the wind, fixed the eyes on that distant grey shape, and started up the trail toward the camp.

choices.label: Climb the camp trail.

nodes.body: The spring was the island's heart, and Wren felt it the moment the ferns parted. A cold clear pool welled up quietly under the green shade, water rising from the rock as if the island itself were breathing it out, and every stream on the whole island began here, at this one still source. Wren knelt and drank, and the cold of it went right through. Whoever had drawn the first ochre map had marked this very place, and standing over it now Wren could finally see how the land fitted together, water running five ways to five shores, the shape of it all suddenly clear. A path climbed on toward the ridge and its camp. Fill the flask and climb toward the camp, trace the streams to understand the whole island, or linger mapping every single one.

choices.label: Fill the flask and climb toward the ridge camp.
Trace the streams to understand the whole island.
Linger mapping every single stream.

nodes.body: Wren set out to trace every stream from the spring, and found the task had no bottom. Each rill split into two smaller ones, and each of those into more, branching and branching down every fold of the hillside, endless. The pencil chased them and never caught up. By the time Wren looked up, the sun had swung well past noon, and the map was a beautiful tangle of blue lines with no coast to hold them together, water without a shore. It was lovely and it was useless, a middle with no edge. Wren rolled it up and headed down at last with the island's streams drawn and the island's shape still bare, humbled by the endlessness of running water.

Wren sat with the streams a while and worked out the island's whole drainage, the way a real surveyor does. Every valley, every shore, every trickle traced back to this one welling pool, the connections coming clear one by one until the whole living web of the island's water lay plain. Wren pencilled it into a clean, sure sketch, spare and true, the kind of underlying frame that would anchor a finished chart and make every later line sit right. It was quiet, satisfying work, the bones of understanding a place. Then the climb to the camp called, up out of the ferns toward the high ground, and Wren rolled the water-map safe and rose to answer it.

choices.label: Climb to the ridge camp with the water mapped.

nodes.body: The path from the spring climbed up out of the ferns onto open ridge, where the grass lay flat and combed under the steady wind and the sky opened wide overhead. Wren leaned into the climb, glad of the height gained. Little by little the ground eased, and the path leveled through a high meadow bright with flowers, sheltered somehow from the worst of the wind, grazed here and there by a few wild goats that lifted their heads to watch Wren pass and then bent again to the grass. It was the gentlest ground of the whole day. Ahead, across the meadow, the camp trail lifted away toward the shelter on the ridge, and Wren crossed toward it.

choices.label: Cross the high meadow toward the camp.

nodes.body: The high meadow was the gentlest ground all day, and Wren walked it gratefully, boots quiet in the soft grass, wild goats grazing unbothered nearby. It was ringed by an old tumbled cairn, a boundary marker some surveyor must have raised long ago, its stones fallen but its purpose still plain. That strange old cairn tugged at Wren's curiosity, the way a made thing always does in a wild place. But the camp trail lifted away on the far side of the meadow, plain and calling, and the day was not long. Cross the meadow to the camp trail and climb on, or step aside to investigate the old boundary cairn and whatever it was set here to mark.

choices.label: Cross the meadow to the camp trail.
Investigate the old boundary cairn.

nodes.body: Wren went to the tumbled cairn and read it. Among the fallen stones lay a flat capstone, and scratched into its weathered face was a survey mark and an arrow, one of Alder's boundary points, set here to fix the very middle of the island. Wren brushed the lichen away and traced the mark with a fingertip. It was a small triumph, a fixed and certain point in all the wide wild ground, the kind of thing a chart is built around, and it tied the whole island together from its center. Alder had stood right here, once, and left this. Note the boundary mark and climb on to the camp, or copy the boundary survey in full and rest here a while.

choices.label: Note the boundary mark and climb to the camp.
Copy the boundary survey in full and rest here.

nodes.body: Wren copied Alder's boundary mark and the careful way it tied the whole island together from its center, proper surveyor's work, exact and true. And then the meadow was so soft, and the sun so warm on the grass, and the goats so peaceful, that resting a moment turned without warning into dozing. Wren woke with a start to a lower sun and a cooler wind, and the loss was plain. The middle of the island was fixed and the edges were bare, and the day had quietly moved on while Wren slept in the flowers. Wren rose stiff and cross with itself and hurried down toward the shore with only half a chart, the boundary mark small comfort now.

Alder's ridge camp was a low stone shelter under grey canvas, and stepping in felt like stepping into a story Wren had been hearing all day. A cot stood along one wall, its blanket folded. A cold stove sat in the corner. And on a plank table, weighted flat with smooth stones against the wind, lay a real chart, half the island drawn on it in a fine, sure, patient hand, the rest left open and waiting for someone to finish. Wren stood over it, hardly breathing. A tin trunk sat closed beneath the cot. And beyond the low doorway, the path climbed on toward the summit and the last of the height. Study Alder's chart at the table, open the tin trunk under the cot, or take the chart and start for the summit.

choices.label: Study Alder's chart at the table.
Open the tin trunk under the cot.
Take Alder's chart and start for the summit.

nodes.body: Alder's chart was honest and unfinished, and Wren bent over it, learning it line by line. The coast was drawn true, the caves marked, the reef and cove fixed with care. But the high ground and the far shore were left blank, and across that blank Alder had written a note in the small sure hand Wren now knew so well. Finish this, it said, and it is yours, mapmaker to mapmaker. It was a passing of the work from one hand to the next, a trust laid down across the years. The tools to do it lay ready on the table beside it. Take up the tools and ready to finish the chart yourself, or simply copy Alder's half chart and call the day done.

choices.label: Take up the tools and ready to finish it.
Simply copy Alder's half chart and call it done.

nodes.body: Wren copied Alder's half chart neatly, line for careful line, and carried it down to the beach at dusk, and it was a fine map, no question. But the blank half was still blank, and the hand that had drawn the true half was still Alder's, not Wren's. When the mate spread it out and ran a thumb to the empty summit, the frown said everything. A copied map is a story, the mate said, not a survey, and a mapmaker is paid to look with their own eyes, not to trace another's. So Wren went home with a fine tale of the lost mapmaker and a beautiful borrowed chart, and no apprenticeship yet, and understood the difference now.

The tin trunk held Alder's whole island life, and Wren lifted the lid slowly, feeling like a guest at something private. Inside lay fine pens ranked in a row, a brass spyglass, a signal mirror bright as new, and a journal, its ribbon marking a place near the end. Wren opened it there and read a line, and the journal simply stopped mid-sentence, cut off the day a storm took the boat that never came back. The spyglass and mirror could reach the distant ship. The pens could finish any chart in the world. And the journal held the rest of the story, waiting. Gather the tools and ready to climb on toward the summit, or read the journal to its last page first.

choices.label: Gather the tools and ready to climb on.
Read the journal to its last page.

nodes.body: Wren read the journal, and it told the whole of it. Alder mapping alone, season after season, loving the island more with every page. Naming the hidden cove. Fixing the spring, the reef, the far wild shore. And at the end, waiting on the ridge for a ship that came too late, the storm rising, the writing growing thin and hopeful and then stopping altogether. The last full line was a wish, plain and open: whoever finds this, finish the chart, and give the island its name. Wren closed the cover gently, the wish settling like a weight and a gift at once. Carry Alder's wish on toward the summit and finish the work, or close the journal, moved, and simply keep it safe.

choices.label: Carry Alder's wish on toward the summit.
Close the journal, moved, and simply keep it.

nodes.body: Wren closed Alder's journal with a lump in the throat and decided, right there, that some things matter more than a test. The whole afternoon went to reading every page, the loneliness and the wonder and the love of it, the long patient life of a mapmaker who gave the island everything and asked only that it be finished and named. When the light failed, Wren rowed out at dusk with a blank chart and a full heart, carrying Alder's story home so the lost mapmaker would not be forgotten. There would be other islands, other days, other chances at the apprenticeship. But there was only one Alder, and Wren would not leave the story on the ridge to fade.

Tools in hand and Alder's half chart rolled safe under one arm, Wren stepped back out of the camp into the wind with everything a mapmaker needed to finish the work. The pens, the glass, the fixed and certain lines already drawn, and the will to add the rest. It felt like readiness, solid and calm. Only one place remained now, the one place high enough to see and draw the whole island at once, coast and cave and ridge and reef together in a single sweep. The summit. It stood above the camp, dark against the racing cloud, close now and waiting. Wren settled the satchel, checked the sky, and set out for the top.

choices.label: Set out for the summit.

nodes.body: Above the camp the path narrowed to a thread of worn grass between two long drops, and Wren slowed and gave it full attention, because ground like this forgives nothing. The summit cairn showed dark ahead against the racing cloud, close now but guarded by the fall on either hand. A line of cairns marked the safe way through, set by a careful hand to keep a climber honest. Off to the side, a bare scree slope offered a shortcut straight up, tempting and quick and treacherous. Wren knew which the cairns would say, and knew too how a tired mind talks itself toward the fast way. Follow the cairns the safe way, or try the scree shortcut straight up.

choices.label: Follow the cairns the safe way.
Try the scree shortcut straight up.

nodes.body: Wren tried the scree shortcut, and the whole slope slid away the moment any weight went onto it, a slow hissing river of loose stones carrying the feet out from under. There was no climbing that. Wren crabbed sideways off it, breathless, scrabbling for the firm edge, and made it back to the safe ground shaken and gasping, the shortcut proven a trap exactly as the cairns had warned. It cost time, and worse, it cost nerve. With the light leaning on and both legs trembling, Wren judged the summit a bridge too far for one day, and the high chart stayed unfinished. Some shortcuts are only the long way to a fall, and Wren had learned to hear the difference.

The cairns led on to a notch in the ridge, a narrow gap in the rock where the wind funnelled through so hard that Wren could lean bodily into it and be held up by nothing but moving air. It was wild and exhilarating and Wren went through it low and braced, one hand on the rock, letting the wind take some of the effort and giving it none of the balance. Beyond the notch the ground opened out to the final rise, and the summit stood close now, close enough to count the stones of its cairn, above one last field of tumbled boulders. Wren steadied against the funnelled wind, caught a breath in the lee of the rock, and crossed toward the boulder field and the rise.

choices.label: Cross the boulder field to the rise.

nodes.body: The boulder field was slow, careful work, hand and foot from block to block, the cloud tearing past in ragged streams, and Wren took it patiently, testing each great stone before trusting it with weight. There was no hurrying such ground and no need to. Block by block, hold by tested hold, the summit drew nearer, until Wren pulled up over the last tumbled boulder and there was nothing higher left to climb. Only the bare crown of the island, and the sky. Wren crouched a moment on the last rock, catching a breath, the whole world falling away below on every side, then gathered the will and the limbs for the final step up onto the very top.

choices.label: Stand up onto the summit.

nodes.body: The summit was a bare cairn of stacked stones standing in a river of cloud, and Wren climbed up onto it and saw, at last, the whole island. Every shore, every reef, every secret cove and dark cave mouth and shining flat, all of it laid out below like a chart already half drawn in the mind, only waiting for the hand to catch up. It took Wren's breath clean away. This was the place to finish the work, the one vantage high enough to see it whole. But the wind up here was fierce, tugging at the paper, and the light already leaned toward afternoon, so every choice from here would count double. Set up to survey the whole island now, signal the far ship first with Alder's mirror, or just stand and take it all in.

choices.label: Set up to survey the whole island now.
Signal the far ship first with Alder's mirror.
Just stand and take it all in.

nodes.body: The view was so vast that Wren simply stared, turning slowly on the cairn, drinking in the whole island and the wide ring of sea beyond, every shore and reef and secret laid out under the racing sky. It was too much and not enough all at once, and Wren could not look away. Then a cold gust and a sudden slant of gold light broke the spell, and the truth of the hour landed hard. There was no time now to draw it properly, only to scramble down before the dark. Wren reached the beach at dusk with a head full of island and a chart still mostly bare, having stood where everything could be seen and drawn none of it.

From the cairn Wren laid Alder's half chart on a flat stone, weighted it against the wind, and began, and the work came faster than seemed possible. Sight the ridge, and mark it. Sight the reef, the cove, the pale flats, and mark them each in turn, the island's true shape coming up under the pencil line by line, the blank half filling at last. It was the finest hour of the whole day. But there was more to fix than the failing light allowed, and a mapmaker must choose what to nail down first. Take careful sightings of every coast, fix the reef and cove for safe landings first, turn the glass on the wild far shore, or try to rush every detail at once.

choices.label: Take careful sightings of every coast.
Fix the reef and cove for safe landings first.
Turn the glass on the wild far shore.
Rush every detail at once.

nodes.body: Wren tried to draw everything at once, and the hurry undid it. Lines smudged together, one bearing bled into the next, and soon Wren could not tell which mark meant which, the page turning to a crowded muddle under the racing hand. There was no time to redraw it, no time to untangle the crossed lines, and the light was going. Wren carried down a chart that was full but wrong in places, honest effort spoiled by haste, and the mate frowned over it a long time before setting it aside. A near miss, the mate said, not unkindly. Which is the hardest kind, because it means the seeing was there and only the patience failed.

ending.title: All at Once, and None of It

nodes.body: Wren worked steadily around the cairn, taking clean careful sightings of every distant point, the salvaged brass rule swinging true from headland to reef to far pale cape. Each bearing fixed, each one noted in its place, the island tightening into its true shape as the marks accumulated, guesswork giving way to certainty. This was the craft at its best, patient and exact, the wind be hanged. When the last far point was fixed, Wren straightened and looked at the gathered bearings with quiet satisfaction. The bones were all there now, every corner pinned down true. It was time to gather the sketches together and draw the finished chart properly, while the light still held.

choices.label: Gather the sketches to draw the final chart.

nodes.body: Wren fixed the reef and the cove first, the safe water where a ship could come and go, drawing in the channels Alder had begun and never lived to finish. Line by careful line the safe way in took shape, the deep water marked, the teeth of the reef laid bare, the calm cove opened to any captain who could read a chart. It was the most useful map on the whole island, the kind that keeps sailors alive. And with it done, there was still a sliver of light left in the west, just enough, perhaps, to push the whole chart further toward the finished masterwork. Push on toward the full masterwork, or stop here with the safe-landing chart done.

choices.label: Push on toward the full masterwork.
Stop with the safe-landing chart done.

nodes.body: Wren rowed out with a chart that showed exactly how to enter the island safely, the reef, the cove, the tides, every hazard and every safe deep line drawn plain and true. It was not the whole island, and Wren knew it. But when the mate spread it out in the failing light and ran a thumb slowly along the clean-drawn channels, something shifted in the weathered face. This, the mate said, is the map that keeps sailors alive, and there is no finer thing a first chart can be. And with a nod, the mate took Wren on. The whole masterwork could wait for next season; the safe way home could not, and Wren had drawn it.

ending.title: The Chart That Brings Ships Home

nodes.body: Sheltered from the wind in the lee of the cairn, Wren spread Alder's chart and the whole day's sketches out together, weighting them with stones, and watched the island come whole. Coast and cave, ridge and reef, spring and shore, every piece Wren had gathered fitting against Alder's careful half like the two halves of one puzzle at last joined. It was all there. Everything seen, everything fixed, everything understood, the work of the mapmaker who came first and the mapmaker who finished it lying side by side and making a single true thing. Only the drawing itself remained now, the final clean chart, the last and best hour of the day. Wren took up a pen and made ready to draw it.

choices.label: Draw the final chart.

nodes.body: Everything was ready, and Wren knew that how the chart was drawn now would decide everything. The seeing was done; the making remained. Wren could ink it bold and clean, dark sure lines that would read at a glance and last for years. Or pencil it soft and fine, laying in every small detail, every tide pool and orchid and cave, a map almost too full. Or copy it twice, the way a careful surveyor keeps a spare against loss, one to keep and one to give. Each way was honest, and each said something different about the mapmaker who chose it. Wren flexed cold fingers over the paper, weighed the three, and settled on how to draw the finished island.

choices.label: Ink the final chart in bold clean lines.
Pencil it soft and add every small detail.
Copy it twice, one to keep and one to give.

nodes.body: Wren inked the chart with Alder's good pen, and it came alive under the nib. Every coastline sure and dark, every headland fixed, the compass rose crisp and clean at the corner, the cove and the reef named at last in a firm clear hand. It was bold and true and unmistakably Wren's own work, the day's whole journey set down permanent in black ink that no fog and no failing memory could ever wash away. Alder's half and Wren's half had become one seamless island, drawn to stay. Wren sat back and looked at it, and it was good, finished and strong. Only one thing remained now. Sign the finished masterwork.

choices.label: Sign the finished masterwork.

nodes.body: Wren pencilled the chart soft and fine, and left nothing out. Every tide pool, every orchid on its rock face, the handprint cave and the star slab and the vanished village, all of it laid in with a patient grey line until the map was almost a portrait of the island, a thing you could fall into and wander. It took longer, far longer, and the light was nearly gone by the time the last small detail went down. But nothing was missing, nothing forgotten, the whole living island held on one crowded loving page. Wren blew the last dust from the paper. Sign the detailed masterwork now, or fuss over one last small detail as the sun sinks.

choices.label: Sign the detailed masterwork.
Fuss over one last detail as the sun sinks.

nodes.body: Wren could not stop adding to the soft pencil map. One more cove, drawn in. One more current, traced. One more tiny cave marked, one more reef, the hand moving and moving while the sun sank to a red rim on the sea and the ship's horn sounded low across the water, once, twice. Wren ran for the beach at last with a wonderful, unfinished thing rolled tight, too detailed ever to call done. The mate took one look and sighed. A map you never finish, the mate said, is no map at all, and pointed out to the waiting ship. Wren climbed aboard with the loveliest half-drawn island in the world and the lesson that done beats perfect, every time.

Wren copied the chart twice by the failing light, working quick and sure now that the seeing was long done, one clean master to give the Chart House and one working copy to keep.
