That was how the real surveyors did it, Wren had learned, a map too precious to trust to a single sheet that one spilled lamp or one gust off a cliff could destroy. So Alder's island was doubled, and made safe from any single stroke of bad luck, two true charts where an hour before there had been none. The pen moved steady in the last of the light. When both were done, Wren laid them side by side and made ready to sign both charts.

choices.label: Sign both charts.

nodes.body: The chart was done, finished and true, and there was one thing left to do, the thing Alder's journal had asked for. The name. Whoever finishes the chart, the journal had said, should name the island, and now a blank banner waited across the top of the page for a single word. Wren held the pen over it and felt the weight of the choosing. A name would outlast Wren, outlast the mate, outlast the ship, spoken by sailors who would never know the island had once been blank. Name it for Alder who came before, name it for both mapmakers together, ring the margins with the island's living things, or leave the banner blank and simply sign the survey.

choices.label: Name it for Alder who came before.
Name it for both mapmakers together.
Ring the margins with the island's living things.
Leave the banner blank and sign the survey.

nodes.body: Wren wrote Alder's Island across the top of the chart in careful letters and rowed out as the first stars came, laying the finished masterwork in the mate's waiting hands. The mate read the name, then read it again, and understood the whole story folded into those two words, the mapmaker who began and the one who finished, the years between, the promise kept. For a long moment the mate said nothing. Then, quietly: the Chart House has its new apprentice. And Alder, the mate added, looking at the name, would be proud that the island has a map at last, and a name to go with it. Wren looked back at the dark island, finished, and felt the day settle into place.

Wren wrote both names along the banner, the one who began the work and the one who finished it, and handed the masterwork across as the light failed. The mate studied the perfect chart, the coast and reef and ridge all true, and then the two names side by side at the top, and was quiet a while. This, the mate said at last, is the finest first survey the House has seen in years, and the naming shows why. A mapmaker who shares the credit is a mapmaker worth training. Wren was an apprentice no longer, but a mapmaker, name earned and island named, the two hands honored together on one true page.

Wren left the banner blank and signed the survey in the corner instead, small and neat, the way Alder always had, and delivered a chart so complete and so true that the mate could not find a single fault in it. The mate checked it corner to corner, twice, then set it down with something like wonder. The name can wait for the House to choose, the mate said, but the place cannot. This is the best survey a first islander has ever brought back to me. And there on the darkening beach, chart in hand, the mate offered Wren the apprenticeship on the spot, on the plain strength of the work itself, which was all Wren had ever wanted it to speak for.

With two finished charts in hand, master and working copy both, Wren faced one last small choice as the boat waited at the water's edge and the mate stood ready in the stern. How to present them. Wren could hand over both together, openly, as plain proof of a surveyor's care and forethought. Or give one and quietly keep the spare, the first sheet of a mapmaker's own private library, a thing held back for oneself. Both were honest. One showed the mate everything; one kept a little for the road ahead. Wren weighed the two rolled charts, one in each hand. Present both to show a surveyor's care, or give one and keep the spare quietly.

choices.label: Present both to show a surveyor's care.
Give one and keep the spare quietly.

nodes.body: Wren handed the mate both charts, master and working copy, exactly as a seasoned surveyor would, and explained the reasoning plainly: a map is too precious to trust to a single sheet, so a careful mapmaker always keeps a spare. The mate turned them over, one and then the other, and looked up with plain surprise. A first islander, thinking like a professional. That, the mate said, is judgement, and judgement is rarer than skill. So Wren won the apprenticeship for the thinking as much as the drawing, and Alder's island went home saved twice over, two true charts against the losses of the world. It was, the mate allowed, a very good day's work.

Wren gave the mate one clean chart and kept the spare tucked quietly away, and the map that changed hands was fine enough on its own to earn a place at the Chart House. The mate spread it, nodded slowly over the true coast and the fixed reef, and welcomed Wren aboard. But the kept copy stayed Wren's own, rolled small and safe, the first sheet of a mapmaker's private library, a thing to keep and learn from and one day pass on. Alder's island was doubled now, one half given to the world and one half quietly, proudly Wren's. It felt like a beginning, which was exactly what it was.

The ship was a far white speck standing off the reef, patient, waiting for dusk, and Wren remembered Alder's signal mirror warm in the satchel. With it, the low sun could be thrown straight across the water at that distant sail. A flash, the old distress rhythm, might call the boat in early to the sheltered cove, and grown hands with it, in time to help the stranded creatures Wren had spotted from the heights, the seal pup in the warm cave, the turtle out on the reef. It would cost the finishing of the chart, or most of it. Aim the mirror and flash the ship, or signal quickly and then turn straight back to finish the chart.

choices.label: Aim the mirror and flash the ship.
Signal, then turn back to finish the chart.

nodes.body: Wren steadied the little mirror against the buffeting wind, both hands cupped around it, and found the angle by patient degrees. The trick was to catch the low sun square on the glass and throw it, and Wren tilted and turned until suddenly the light took, a bright hard blade of it leaping off the mirror and out across the water toward the distant sail. There it was, caught and aimed. Wren held the wrists locked, fighting the wind that wanted to jog the beam off true, keeping the flash pointed at that far white speck with everything the arms had. Now it was a matter of holding steady, and waiting, and hoping the far ship was watching the island as closely as Wren was watching it.

choices.label: Hold the flash steady and wait.

nodes.body: Flash, pause, flash, the old distress rhythm drilled into every Chart House apprentice until the hands knew it without the head. Wren worked the mirror in the pattern, tilting the bright blade on and off across the miles of water. And for a long while, nothing. Only the wind, and the far white sail sitting motionless, and the doubt creeping in cold at the edges. Maybe no one watched. Maybe the flash was lost in the glare. Wren's arm began to ache, then to burn, holding the mirror true against the wind. But a signaller's whole job is to keep on past the doubt, so Wren set the jaw and kept signalling, flash, pause, flash, refusing to be the first to quit.

choices.label: Keep signalling.

nodes.body: At last the far sail shifted. Wren, arm trembling, hardly dared believe it, and then it was unmistakable: the ship swung square, hung a moment, and heeled over as it came about, bringing its bow around toward the island. Answering. Steering for the sheltered cove, in early, called by a blade of light off a dead mapmaker's mirror. Wren let out a shout that the wind tore away and lowered the aching arm. It had worked. Somewhere out there a lookout had seen the flash and understood, and the boat was coming. Now the whole thing turned on speed. Wren had to get down off the summit and reach the cove in time to meet it. Race down toward the cove to meet the boat.

choices.label: Race down toward the cove to meet the boat.

nodes.body: Wren pocketed the mirror and went down the ridge at a controlled run, fast but never reckless, cairn to cairn the way the safe path went, because a fall now would undo everything. Past the camp, down the cut cove path, boots hammering the worn steps, lungs burning, the wind pushing and the light going gold. Down and down, the cove rising to meet each stride, and below, small and growing, the ship's little boat pulled in through the gap in the reef, oars flashing, sailors bending to the stroke. Wren pressed on, minding the footing even at speed, everything now a matter of reaching the water before the boat grounded. Meet the boat at the cove.

choices.label: Meet the boat at the cove.

nodes.body: The boat grounded on the cove sand, and two strong sailors jumped out, sent in by the mate who had seen the signal and understood it. There was just time before the returning tide to help, and Wren, having read the whole coast from the heights above, knew exactly where. A young seal pup lay stranded in the warm cave below, wriggled in from the sea and unable to climb out. And out on the reef, a small turtle waited on its back in a draining pool, righted by no one yet. Wren could lead the sailors to one before the water rose. Lead them to the seal pup in the cave, or lead them to the turtle on the reef.

choices.label: Lead them to the seal pup in the cave.
Lead them to the turtle on the reef.

nodes.body: Wren guided the two sailors to the warm cave, and together, three pairs of hands where one had failed, they lifted the seal pup gently up and out through the sea tube on the rising tide. It slipped from their arms into the green water and was gone, whole and free, a dark shape darting for the open sea. The sailors laughed. And the mate, come ashore now and watching Wren work, said something Wren would keep for the rest of a long career. Any mapmaker who reads a coast well enough to save a life, the mate said, is worth keeping. So Wren rowed home an apprentice at last, with a good chart and a better story, and both had been earned.

ending.title: The Pup and the Rising Tide

nodes.body: Wren led the sailors out across the flats to the reef, timing it against the tide, and together they carried the stranded turtle down to deep water and set it free. It hung a moment in the green, gathering itself, then kicked once and was gone into the blue. The sailors cheered. The mate, watching from the sand, nodded slowly and said the thing Wren would remember longest. A person who uses a map to help, the mate said, and not only to win, has the makings of a true mapmaker. The chart was only half finished, and it did not matter. The mate offered Wren the place, and Wren took it, and the turtle swam on toward the deep unknowing water.

The still green lagoon was a mirror roofed over by mangroves, so quiet that the plop of a single fish sounded loud as a stone. Wren waded to the edge and looked. Three things waited here. Against the roots lay a half-sunk dugout canoe, old and heavy, filling slowly with rain and leaves. A tunnel of arching roots led off deeper into the green shadow, low enough to make a person stoop. And at the far end of the lagoon, a great heap of driftwood dammed the water, packed and woven as if by hand. Each way promised a different corner of the coast. Wren stood in the warm shallows and chose which to try first.

choices.label: Bail out the sunken canoe.
Take the root tunnel deeper.
Wade to the driftwood dam.

nodes.body: Wren dragged the dugout half onto the mud and looked it over. It was old but sound, carved from a single log by patient hands a long time ago, and now half full of leaves and rainwater. Bailed out, it might carry Wren swiftly across the lagoon and let the true shape of the shore be traced from the open water. Or the old wood might have gone soft and treacherous, and fold and sink under the very first stroke of a paddle. There was a careful way to do this, testing it in the shallows first, and a reckless way, trusting it out into the deep. Wren knelt by the hull and decided.

choices.label: Bail it and paddle across.
Trust it too far, too fast.

nodes.body: Wren pushed the dugout out and dug in a paddle, too eager, too far from shore. The old wood took water at once, a slow gurgle rising through a seam Wren had not seen. Down it went, gently, until Wren sat waist-deep in the warm lagoon with the swamped canoe settling underneath. The sketchbook was saved, held high and dry over one shoulder, and that was the one small victory. Everything else was loss. Wren waded back to the bank streaming water, and the morning was gone with the canoe. That corner of the coast stayed a wet blank, and Wren wrung out both sleeves and let it be.

Bailed dry and balanced with care, the old dugout floated sweetly, and Wren paddled out onto the glassy lagoon. From the water everything made better sense. The true line of the shore showed clear from here, every root and inlet laid out plain, far easier to trace than it had been from the tangled bank. Wren sketched as the canoe drifted, quick sure strokes of the pencil. Across the lagoon a strip of old cleared ground came into view along the far side, level and open, too neat to be entirely wild. Wren turned the bow toward it and paddled for the far shore to see what had cleared it.

choices.label: Land and explore the cleared ground.

nodes.body: Wren beached the canoe and stepped onto the cleared strip. It was too neat to be wild, and the more Wren looked the plainer that became. Someone had shaped this ground once, long ago. Among the trees stood a low mound of stones and a scatter of weathered posts, the ghost of something built and then abandoned. Beyond it, a firm path climbed away from the water, worn by feet, leading up toward the creek and the higher ground of the island. Wren stood between the two, curious. The old stones asked to be understood, and the firm path promised a way up and on. Either could earn a place on the map.

choices.label: Examine the stone mound and posts.
Take the firm path up toward the creek.

nodes.body: Wren walked among the stones and read them slowly, and the shape of the place came clear. The low mound was a fish drying rack, and the posts were the frame of a house, and together they were a whole fishing camp, silent now and swallowed by green. It was older than Alder, older than any chart, older than the Chart House itself perhaps. People had lived on this island once, dried their catch here, raised their children here, and then gone, leaving only this quiet clearing behind. Wren felt the weight of it. There was the choice to note the vanished village and climb on, or to sit and map every post and stone of it in loving detail.

choices.label: Record the vanished village and climb on.
Sit and map the whole village in loving detail.

nodes.body: Wren sat down among the old posts and mapped the vanished camp, stone by stone, rack by rack, imagining the families who had dried their catch here and watched their fires smoke against the trees. The pencil filled page after page. It was careful, honest, loving work, the kind that keeps a lost thing from being wholly lost. But it was one small clearing on a wide island, and when Wren finally looked up, the sun had climbed and the chart as a whole had barely begun. Wren went down to the beach at dusk carrying a village instead of a coastline, and thought it a fair trade, though the mate might not.

The root tunnel was a green throat that Wren had to duck and wade to enter, cool and dim and loud with steady dripping. It was easy to feel small in there, roots arching close on every side. The passage bent, then forked. One way brightened ahead toward a patch of open sky, the promise of climbing out into the light. The other bored deeper, darker, boring on under the very edge of the island where no light reached at all. Wren stood in the cool water where the ways split and listened to the drip. Bright and up, or dark and in. One of them led out, and the other was only a guess.

choices.label: Head for the brightening sky.
Push deeper into the dark.

nodes.body: Wren pushed deeper into the dark, and the tunnel answered by branching, again and again, root passage into root passage until every way looked like every other. The dripping swallowed all sense of direction. When Wren finally admitted the truth, heart thudding, there was nothing to do but backtrack by feel, one hand on the slick roots, toward the last remembered patch of light. It took a long, frightening while. Wren came out at last into the green shade of the lagoon, shaken and dripping, and left the whole tangled corner as a rough scribble. Some places, Wren decided, keep their secrets, and a mapmaker learns to leave them and climb toward open ground.

The driftwood dam was otter work, years of it, a great woven heap of branches holding the water back at the far end of the lagoon. Wren waded close to study how it fit together. Jammed among the branches, weathered grey but standing upright, was a survey stake with a faded number burned into its side. Wren's breath caught. It was one of Alder's marks, driven here long ago and still keeping its place, exactly where an old chart would have said it should be. It felt like proof that the island could be known, that someone careful had come before and left a trail. From the stake, the creek ran on inland, a clear line to follow up toward the higher ground.

choices.label: Note Alder's stake and follow the creek.
Prise the stake free as a keepsake.

nodes.body: Wren worked the old survey stake loose from the otters' dam and turned it over, reading the faded number burned into the grain. Holding the real thing, the actual wood Alder had driven into this bank, made the whole day feel like Alder's more than Wren's own. It was a relic, and relics are heavy in more ways than one. Wren carried it home like treasure and barely lifted the pencil after, the map hardly started, the stake worth more just then than a hundred clean lines. It was not the chart the mate had asked for. But it was a piece of the mapmaker who came first, and Wren would not trade it back.

Wren followed the roar until the trees opened on a waterfall, white water pouring off a mossy lip into a green pool loud with darting kingfishers. Spray drifted cool across Wren's face, and the whole clearing rang with the sound of it. Three ways offered themselves around the fall. A wet rock scramble climbed steeply beside the plunging water, slick but quick for careful hands. A mossy log crossed the stream above the very lip, a bridge for a steady nerve. And in the churning pool below, pale shapes showed under the foam, something sunk down there and worth a closer look. Wren stood in the drifting spray and weighed the roar, the height, and the depth.

choices.label: Climb the wet rocks beside the falls.
Cross the mossy log above the lip.
Wade into the pool below.

nodes.body: The rocks beside the falls were slick with spray and furred with moss, but under the green Wren's fingers found good solid holds, ledges and cracks worn by the water. It was a fast way up to the traverse for anyone who kept a cool head. Careful climbing, testing each hold before trusting it, would carry Wren safely to the top. But there was a reckless way too, scrambling up quick and heedless, trusting to luck instead of grip, and on wet rock that was how a person ended up in the pool with a bruise and a soaking. Wren pressed a boot against the first hold, felt it hold firm, and decided how to climb.

choices.label: Climb carefully, testing each hold.
Scramble up fast and reckless.

nodes.body: Wren scrambled up too fast, too sure, and a mossy hold peeled away under one hand with a soft wet tear. There was a lurch, a rush of cold air, and then Wren slid down the streaming rock into the pool below in a burst of bubbles and spray. Nothing was broken, but everything was soaked, and the cold drove the breath right out. Wren surfaced gasping, hauled to the bank, and sat shivering on a stone. The sketchbook had been held high and stayed dry, the one saving grace. But the climbing was over for now, and the high ridge stayed unmapped above, its chart lost to a moment of hurry on wet green rock.

The mossy log lay broad across the stream above the lip of the fall, and Wren tested it with a boot before trusting it, pressing down hard to be sure it would not roll or give. It held, surprisingly firm under the moss. Wren stepped out onto it, arms wide for balance, and edged across slowly. Looking down was a thrill and a warning both, straight along the falling white water to the pool far below. Wren kept the eyes on the far bank instead, the safe trick every climber learns. Step by careful step, the log carried across to solid ground, where the traverse contoured away toward the upper ridge and the higher country beyond.

choices.label: Step off onto the upper traverse.

nodes.body: Wren waded into the shallows at the edge of the pool, minding the slick footing, and looked at the pale shapes below the churn. They were not fish, but stone. A flat cut slab lay sunk on the bottom, its surface pecked with a scale of small even notches climbing one side. Wren understood it after a moment, and the understanding was a small joy. It was a water gauge, set here on purpose to measure how high the falls rose and fell through the seasons, marked notch by careful notch. Alder's hand again, patient and exact. Wren could note the gauge quickly and climb the falls, or copy the whole clever thing down in full.

choices.label: Note the gauge and climb the falls.
Copy the gauge stone in full detail.

nodes.body: Wren copied Alder's water gauge notch by notch, working out from the marks how the falls swelled in the wet months and shrank in the dry, the slow breathing of the island's water through a whole year. It was real science, the patient kind that turns a place from a picture into something truly understood. The pencil moved and the hours slipped by with it. When it was done, the page was a small marvel, and the island around it was still mostly blank. Wren climbed out of the pool at last, damp and thoughtful, carrying home a piece of Alder's patient study and leaving the coastline for another, quicker day.

A bright hole of daylight hung high in the cavern roof, where a shaft had broken through to the surface long ago, and beneath it a slope of fallen rock climbed up toward the light. It was the clearest way out Wren had seen, a ladder of tumbled stone reaching for the sky. But off to one side, another wonder glowed softly in the dark. A gallery of stone formations spread away there, hung with pale drapes and columns of rock, the slow work of dripping water over uncounted years. Wren stood between them, the honest climb toward the daylight and the strange glowing hall to the side, and weighed the pull of each.

choices.label: Climb the rock slope toward the light.
Explore the gallery of formations.

nodes.body: The last stretch up to the daylight was a narrow chimney of rock, and Wren studied it before committing. The trick was to climb it with the back braced against one wall and the boots pressed against the other, inching upward by main strength, a hard bright squeeze that would pop out onto the open hillside, high and quick, if the arms and legs held. It was a real climb, not a scramble. Done steadily, testing each press before shifting weight, it would go. Rushed, with arms tiring too soon, it would leave a person stuck halfway with nothing below but the cavern floor. Wren wedged in, braced, and thought hard about pace before starting up.

choices.label: Chimney up steadily to the surface.
Rush the chimney and tire too soon.

nodes.body: Wren rushed the chimney, and halfway up the arms began to shake, the strength draining faster than the daylight came closer. This was the moment that decides such things. Wren did not push on and hope, because hope is a poor grip on rock. Instead, carefully, boots and back working together, Wren slid back down to the cavern floor before a slip could turn into a fall. It was the right call and it stung all the same. The surface stayed out of reach by this route, and now another way up had to be found, with time bleeding quietly away. Wren stood at the bottom, arms burning, and started the search again.

The gallery was a frozen forest of stone, and Wren walked into it slowly, breath held. Columns rose where drips had met over ages, thin and pale, and drapes of rock hung folded like curtains turned to stone. It was beautiful and strange and utterly still. Among the columns lay the small bleached bones of a goat that had fallen in long ago and never found its way out. And beside the bones, half sunk in the mineral crust, lay a rusted lantern, proof that some human searcher had come this way before, hunting for something in the dark. Wren could follow where that searcher had gone, on toward a glitter deeper in, or stay and sketch the whole silent gallery.

choices.label: Follow where the searcher went, toward the glitter.
Sketch the whole stone gallery.

nodes.body: Wren drew the stone gallery until the page glowed with columns and hanging drapes, every frozen fold of it set down in careful line, and tucked the rusted lantern into the corner of the sketch like a signature left by the past. It was beautiful work, the kind that makes a viewer forget to breathe. But it was one dark room deep underground, and the sunlit island above, its coasts and ridges, stayed only a rumour on the blank half of the paper. When Wren finally climbed back toward the daylight, it was with a cave carried home in the sketchbook and a coastline left undrawn. A gallery, and not a chart.

The keeper's side quarters were small and sad and deeply human, and stepping in Wren went quiet. A narrow bunk stood against one wall, its blanket still folded. A cold hearth held old grey ash. At the foot of the bunk sat a sea chest, latched shut, and the whole opposite wall was pinned with charts, dozens of them, gone soft and curling at the edges with the damp. This was where the mapmaker had actually lived, between the great works, an ordinary little room at the top of the world. Wren felt like an intruder and a guest at once. Read the wall of pinned charts, or open the sea chest at the foot of the bunk.

choices.label: Read the wall of pinned charts.
Open the sea chest at the bunk's foot.

nodes.body: Wren read the wall of pinned charts, and slowly understood what it was. These were not this island at all. They were a dozen others, coasts and reefs and bays from all across the wide sea, each one drawn in the same small sure hand, each signed in the same corner. It was a whole life of mapmaking pinned to one wall, Alder's life, island after island found and fixed and named. The scale of it took Wren's breath. One person, patiently drawing the edges of the world, year after year. Wren could take heart from that long labor and climb the tower stair to add to it, or stay and study the wall of a mapmaker's entire life.

choices.label: Take heart from Alder's life and climb the stair.
Study the wall of a mapmaker's whole life.

nodes.body: Wren stood a long time before Alder's wall of charts, reading a lifetime of islands, coast after distant coast, each fixed by one patient hand. It was an education worth more than any single test, this quiet gallery of a life spent drawing the edges of the sea. Wren copied a few of the finest charts into the sketchbook to carry home and learn from, tracing the sure lines with something like reverence. But the sun does not wait for wonder, and while Wren studied, the day slid on. Wren rowed out at dusk with Alder's whole story in the sketchbook and this one island still blank, and did not feel it a wasted day at all.

The sea chest opened on the small treasures of a life away from home. A folded oilskin coat, stiff with salt. A brass pocket compass that, when Wren nudged it, still swung true to north. And beneath them, carefully kept, a child's crayon drawing of a house and three small figures standing outside it, holding hands. That stopped Wren cold. This was the family Alder had left ashore to come and map the world, kept close in a chest at the top of a lonely tower. The compass could serve Wren well on the climb ahead. But the drawing asked to be sat with. Take the working compass and climb on, or stay a while with the child's picture, undone by it.

choices.label: Take the working compass and climb on.
Sit with the child's drawing, undone.

nodes.body: The crayon drawing undid Wren completely, a house and three little people waiting outside it, drawn by a child's hand for a parent who went to sea to map the world and never came home again. Wren sat down with it in the quiet room, and the morning went to imagining that family, their waiting, their not-knowing. It was impossible to hurry after that. Wren tucked the little drawing carefully away, meaning to return it one day to whoever might still be waiting, and rowed out at dusk with a heart full of Alder's story and a chart still bare. Some things a mapmaker cannot draw, and this was one of them.

The mussel beds were a black glistening field that crunched underfoot, sharp enough that Wren picked each step with care, minding both boots on the cutting shells. Oystercatchers worked the beds, prising and probing, bright-billed and busy. Beyond the mussels, a colony of seals had hauled out on a low rock to bask, their wet backs shining, and off to one side a deep gully cut a dark line straight through the flats toward the cliffs, a shortcut of a kind. Wren stood among the crunching shells and weighed the two. The seals drew the eye and the heart. The gully drew a quicker line inland. But a deep gully on a flat means fast, deep water when the tide turns.

choices.label: Approach the basking seals.
Follow the deep gully toward the cliffs.

nodes.body: The seals watched Wren with huge dark eyes and did not flee, a whole colony of them, mothers and fat pups, hauled up to rest and warm on the sun-heated rock. Wren stopped a respectful distance off, not wanting to spook them, and simply looked, learning them for the map's margin. Then one small pup, apart from the rest, caught the eye. It lay struggling, a loop of old fishing net snarled around one flipper, tugging and unable to work free. Wren's chest tightened. Try to free it now, and the whole colony might stampede off the rock in fright. Better, perhaps, to mark the tangled pup carefully so grown hands could be sent to help, and read the shoreline on. Or try, gently, alone.

choices.label: Mark the tangled pup to send help later.
Try to free the tangled pup yourself now.

nodes.body: Wren crept close, slow and low, and worked the old net gently off the pup's flipper until it came loose and the pup humped away to its mother, free. That was a good thing, a real one. But the whole colony spooked at the movement, and in a heaving rush of bodies and barking they flooded off the rock and into the water, gone in seconds. The fright and the fuss had eaten the tide's low window, the safe crossing time, and Wren looked up to find the flats already glazing over with returning water. A good deed done and paid for. Wren waded back to shore proud and behind, the pup saved and the flats left half drawn, and counted it worth the cost.

The deep gully cut a shortcut straight toward the cliffs, its floor still just dry, and Wren stood at its mouth reading it before stepping in. The walls told the true story. Weed streaked them well above Wren's own head, dark tide-lines marking how deep and how fast this narrow place flooded when the sea turned. A shortcut, yes, but a trap if the timing went wrong. The smart move was to hurry through now, while the floor was dry, and not to dawdle. But the weed lines were so clear a record of the tides that they tempted a mapmaker to stop and read them properly, right there in the bottom of the gully, which was exactly the wrong place to linger.

choices.label: Hurry through the gully to the cliff gap.
Dawdle reading the weed lines too long.

nodes.body: Wren lingered too long in the gully, puzzling over the weed lines, working out the tides from the marks on the walls, and forgot the first rule of the place. The first cold tongue of returning water slid up the gully floor and licked at both boots. That woke Wren fast. There was nothing to do but splash back the way in, quick and undignified, and take the long safe road around instead, the shortcut lost to the sea. Wren scrambled clear onto higher ground, heart thumping, and looked back at the gully already filling behind. A good stretch of the flats went unmapped, the day slipping on, all for reading the tides in the one spot the tides could reach.

Wren turned the brass glass on the far shore last of all, the wild windward coast where no boat ever dared to land, and drew what no chart had ever held. The sheer cliffs. The sea stacks standing off them like broken teeth. The long white line of a reef guarding a coast that had kept itself secret since the island rose from the sea. Wren sketched it all through the swaying glass, hardly breathing, fixing ground that had never been mapped by anyone. It was the rarest work of the whole day, the finding of the truly unfound. Add the far shore to the survey and gather the whole thing together, or stop here, thrilled, having charted the unknown coast.

choices.label: Add the far shore and gather the whole survey.
Stop, thrilled, having charted the unknown coast.

nodes.body: Wren carried down a chart of the wild windward shore alone, the one coast no mapmaker had ever reached, the cliffs and stacks and guardian reef all drawn true from the summit glass. The rest of the island was only sketched, rough and unfinished. But when the mate spread the page in the failing light and saw that unknown coast set down plain, the mate whistled low through the teeth. It was not the masterwork, and both of them knew it. Yet it was real new ground, wrested from a shore that had hidden since the world began. A surveyor who finds the unfindable, the mate said, is worth training, and kept Wren on for the daring of it.

ending.title: The Coast No One Had Mapped

nodes.body: The masterwork was drawn and signed, the little boat waiting below, and Wren had one last choice as the light went gold across the page. Hand the chart over now, clean and certain, sure of the work. Or take a few more minutes to ring its margins with the island's living things, the seals that basked on its rocks and the turtles of its reef, the vanished fishing camp, the star slab above the cove, so the map would hold not just the shape of the island but its life, all the wonders a bare survey leaves out. Both were true to the day. Add the living island to the margins, or hand over the clean masterwork just as it is.

choices.label: Add the living island to the margins.
Hand over the clean masterwork as it is.

nodes.body: Wren ringed the finished chart with tiny true drawings, a seal pup and a turtle at the shore, the vanished fishing camp among its trees, the star slab tilted above the cove, so the map held not just the island's shape but its whole living self. The mate turned it slowly in the last of the light, taking in the margins, the small careful wonders crowded around the sure coastline. This is more than a survey, the mate said quietly. It is the island itself, on paper. And there on the darkening beach the mate gave Wren the apprenticeship, and something more besides, the honour of naming the next island the House sent a mapmaker to find.

Wren handed over the clean inked masterwork exactly as drawn, coast and reef and ridge all true, no flourish, no fuss, nothing but the honest shape of the island fixed forever in black ink. The mate took it and checked it corner to corner, slowly, the way a mate checks a thing that matters, and found not one fault in it anywhere. A clean honest survey, the mate said at last, folding it with care, is what the Chart House lives on. Not the fancy ones. The true ones. And on the strength of one perfect first island, drawn plain and drawn right, Wren was taken on as the newest apprentice mapmaker in the House, which was the whole of the dream.

Wren lashed a raft of reeds and poled across to the heron island, a low green mound white with nesting birds and loud as a market square. The noise was tremendous, the smell worse, but a mapmaker goes where the land is. Three features stood out from the water. A weathered watching blind perched on stilts above the reeds, built by someone who wanted to sit and observe. A wide mud bank shelved off one side, crowded with wading birds probing the ooze. And the treetops seethed with the whole rookery, nest upon nest, wings never still. Wren tied off the raft and considered which part of this loud green place to explore.

choices.label: Climb into the old watching blind.
Wade out onto the wide mud bank.
Count the nests in the rookery.

nodes.body: The blind was a surveyor's hide, and Alder had used it well. Wren climbed up into the shade of it and found the rails scratched all over with careful work. There was a whole tide table cut into the wood, hour by hour, and beside it the compass bearings of every headland in sight, each one named in a small neat hand. It was a place built to sit still and let the island slowly give up its shape, the patient heart of the mapmaker's craft. Wren copied what mattered most, then looked for the way on. A plank walk ran off the back of the blind toward firmer ground and the seep that climbed inland.

choices.label: Copy the bearings and take the plank walk inland.
Copy the entire carved tide table in full.

nodes.body: Wren copied Alder's whole carved tide table, hour by patient hour, every mark of when each flat and channel flooded and drained. It was a real treasure, the kind of knowledge that would make some future chart wonderful, safe, alive to the moods of the sea. The morning went into it gladly. But when the last number was down, Wren looked up to a risen sun and remembered the rest of the island, still unseen. One rail of scratched figures, however fine, was not a map of anything but time. Wren poled reluctantly back across the water, carrying Alder's tides home and leaving the island's shape for another day.

The mud bank shelved wide and shining off the island's edge, crowded with wading birds that stitched the ooze with their long bills. Wren studied it before stepping out. A firm ribbon of packed sand threaded across the mud like a path laid down on purpose, leading toward a far channel and the higher ground beyond it. Step onto that ribbon and the crossing was safe. Step off it, into the soft shining mud between, and a person went in to the knee and worse, grasped and held. The birds lifted and settled, lifted and settled. Wren judged the firm line with care, because the safe way and the quick way were not the same way here.

choices.label: Follow the firm ribbon to the far channel.
Cut straight across and startle the flock.

nodes.body: Wren cut straight across the soft mud instead of keeping to the firm ribbon, and the whole bank answered at once. A thousand birds exploded upward together in a roaring storm of wings, so sudden and so loud that Wren flinched, staggered, and went in knee-deep to the grasping mud. By the time the flock wheeled away and Wren had floundered clear, plastered head to boot, laughing and shaking at the same time, the tide had begun to turn. There was no fixing it now. The heron island went onto the paper as a hasty smudge, and Wren waded back to the raft, a lesson learned about crossing a bird bank in a hurry.

The firm ribbon of sand carried Wren dry-shod all the way across the mud to the far channel, just as it had promised. Here the water ran clear and cool, curling toward the distant mainland shore, and beyond it the ground lifted at last, green and solid, out of the reach of any tide. A low rise showed ahead, the kind of lookout a mapmaker dreams of, high enough to see the whole northern coast spread out below. Wren waded the shallow channel, boots squelching, and started up toward the rise, glad to be leaving the soft and shining places behind for honest ground.

choices.label: Cross to the lookout rise.

nodes.body: Wren waded in among the nests, counting them for the map's margin, careful not to knock a single one. Down in the trampled grass a fallen chick lay cheeping, too young to fly, blown or bumped from a nest above. Wren cupped it gently and set it back on a low branch beneath its parents, who glared down cross-eyed and furious until the chick was safe. Then Wren stepped back and sketched the whole seething colony, the wheeling parents, the packed nests, the endless racket of it. It was a living page, full of noise and wings. From here a way led on across the mud bank toward the higher, quieter ground.

choices.label: Sketch the rookery and cross to the mud bank.
Stay to watch the whole colony too long.

nodes.body: Wren could not stop watching the herons. The fishing, the squabbles over nesting sticks, the fallen chick settling safe at last beneath its glaring parents, all of it held the eye and filled the sketchbook with birds instead of coastline. It was a lovely, living page, the island breathing on paper. But the sun climbed on, unmapped and indifferent, while Wren watched. When the light finally said how late it was, the rookery was drawn and the coast was not. Wren poled slowly back across the water, a colony of herons captured whole and an island still waiting for its shape, and did not much regret the choice.

The grove was a cathedral of giant moss-hung trees, and stepping into it Wren fell quiet without meaning to. The light came down green and holy through the high leaves, and the air was still and warm after the buffeting ridge wind, as though the wind knew better than to enter here. Two things drew the eye among the great trunks. A frayed rope ladder climbed away into the high canopy, tied off long ago, swaying faintly. And deep among the roots stood a small cairn shrine, a knee-high pile of stones dressed with pale shells, plainly made by careful, reverent hands. Wren stood in the green hush, torn between the height above and the quiet mystery below.

choices.label: Climb the old rope ladder into the canopy.
Kneel to the little cairn shrine.

nodes.body: The rope ladder was grey and frayed, and Wren tested it rung by rung before trusting any weight to it, the way a climber must. The lower rungs held firm, one after another, and above the leaves there had to lie a view over the whole ridge, worth the risk to reach. But the higher rungs looked thinner, more worn, the fibers gone soft with years of damp. Trusting them fully would be a real gamble. Wren gripped the ladder and weighed it, the sensible climb where every rung is tested against the reckless rush where a rotten rung might part. High places reward care and punish haste, and Wren knew which this was.

choices.label: Climb steadily, testing every rung.
Trust the frayed upper rungs and hurry.

nodes.body: Wren trusted the frayed upper rungs and hurried, and a high one parted with a soft rotten crack. For one stomach-lurching moment Wren dropped, boots kicking at air, before the next rung down caught and held. That was enough, more than enough. Heart hammering, Wren climbed carefully back down, one tested hand under another, and stood at last on the safe green floor of the grove, legs shaking. The canopy view was lost, no arguing with a ladder that could not be trusted. The grove went onto the map as a green guess from the ground, and Wren was simply glad to have both feet on the roots again.

The ladder topped out on a plank platform lashed high in the crown of the greatest tree, and Wren pulled up onto it breathless and amazed. This was Alder's treetop survey point. A fixed brass sighting ring was still bolted to the trunk, green with age but true, set here to fix bearings on the country all around. And what country it was. Through gaps in the leaves the whole green ridge rolled away to the distant shine of the sea, the coast and the reef and the far pale flats all laid out below. Wren steadied against the trunk, took careful sightings through the ring, and marked them down before the long climb back to the trail.

choices.label: Take bearings from the platform and climb down to the trail.
Linger in the swaying green crown too long.

nodes.body: The swaying green world of the canopy was so wonderful that Wren stayed, and stayed, sketching the ridge through every gap in the leaves, the sea a distant shine between the branches. Up here the tree moved gently underfoot, and time came unstuck the way it does in beautiful places. It was only when the light changed color that Wren understood how late it had grown. It was a rare view, one that few people ever see and fewer still get to draw. But it was one treetop on one tree, and Wren climbed down at last with a lovely fragment in the sketchbook and no finished chart to carry home.

Wren knelt by the little cairn shrine and looked at it closely. It was a knee-high pile of balanced stones, dressed with pale shells and topped by a single carved wooden bird, its shape worn soft and grey by years of weather. This was older than Alder, older than any chart, a quiet marker left by the island's first people to bless the high forest. Wren felt the weight of all those years, and of the hands that had set each shell in place. It asked to be remembered gently. There was the choice to record the shrine and climb on toward the camp, or to sit a while in the green hush before it.

choices.label: Record the shrine and climb on toward the camp.
Sit long in the green hush before the shrine.

nodes.body: Wren sat a long peaceful while before the little shrine, drawing the shell-dressed cairn and the worn wooden bird atop it, feeling the presence of all the people who had loved this island long before any mapmaker came. The grove emptied Wren of hurry, gently, the way a quiet place will. It was lovely, and it was costly, both at once. The green hush held Wren fast, and the day slipped by outside it, and when the light finally called the hour, only the shrine was set down on the paper. Wren rose stiff and calm, bowed once to the little bird, and carried the stillness home in place of a map.

The passage opened without warning onto an underground lake, black and perfectly still, its far shore lost somewhere in the dark beyond the reach of any light. The silence was enormous. Wren's smallest sound came back doubled off the unseen roof. On a little beach of pale sand at the water's edge sat a raft of old lashed planks, drawn up and waiting, left by some earlier explorer. Overhead the ceiling glittered faintly where seeping water had carried the crystal down and hung it in the dark. Wren stood on the pale sand between the still black water and the glittering roof. Pole the old raft across, or search the strange pale beach first.

choices.label: Pole the old raft across the black lake.
Search the pale sand beach.

nodes.body: The raft floated when Wren eased it out onto the black water, riding low but true, and a long pole found the bottom easily, so a crossing was possible, slow and eerie over the glassy dark. Wren tested the lashings first, tugging at the old cords that held the planks together. They held, for now. Crossing would work if done slowly and evenly, weight kept low and centered, the pole worked with patience. But to hurry, to lean, to push off hard, was to invite the old lashings to give and pitch a person into a cold, dark, unseen swim. Wren balanced on the raft, gripped the pole, and decided how to make the crossing.

choices.label: Pole across slow and steady.
Push off hard and pole in haste.

nodes.body: Wren pushed off too hard and poled in haste, and a lashing gave with a wet snap. The raft tipped, and suddenly Wren was waist-deep in the cold black lake, gasping at the shock of it, hands scrabbling for the pitching planks. There was no bottom to stand on, only cold and dark and the raft slipping away. Wren caught it, kicked hard, and hauled it and the precious dry sketchbook back to the pale beach by feel more than sight, teeth chattering. The crossing was finished before it began. The far dark of the cave kept its secrets, and Wren wrung out both sleeves on the sand and gave the black water the respect it had just demanded.

The far shore was a wonder that made the whole slow crossing worth it. In the shallows hung blind white fish, ghost-pale, drifting in water that had never once seen the sun, their eyes no more than dark smudges. And rising from the sand, a natural stair of flowstone climbed away in smooth stone ripples toward a distant seam of grey daylight high in the rock, a real way up and out. Wren stood between the two marvels, the pale drifting fish and the climbing stone stair. The daylight called upward toward the surface and the map still to be made, while the blind fish hung in their dark water, asking to be understood.

choices.label: Climb the flowstone stair toward the daylight.
Stay to study the blind white fish.

nodes.body: Wren stayed to study the blind white fish, watching them drift and turn in water that had never known daylight, and filled a page with creatures no chart had ever shown, pale and eyeless and strange. It was patient, wondering work, the kind of discovery that would make the whole Chart House lean in and gasp. But it was a single dark pool at the bottom of the island, and the sunlit world above, its coasts and its ridge, waited unmapped while the hours drained quietly away. When Wren finally climbed the flowstone stair toward the daylight, it was with a page of ghost fish and an island still blank. A wonder, and not a survey.

The pale sand held prints, and stooping close with a held breath, Wren saw what they were and felt the years yawn open underfoot. They were footprints, small and bare, the steps of children who had walked this hidden beach an age ago. But they were not soft in the sand. They had turned to stone, hardened over unthinkable time until the deep itself preserved each little toe and heel. Children had played on this dark shore once, long before any mapmaker, and left their steps behind them forever. Wren could record the stone footprints and climb out toward the ridge, or stay and map every ancient step on the beach.

choices.label: Record the stone footprints and climb out to the ridge.
Map every ancient footprint on the beach.

nodes.body: Wren mapped every stone footprint on the hidden beach, each little heel and set of toes, imagining the children who had made them here in the dark so unthinkably long ago. What had they seen? What had they laughed at, down here where no sun reached? The wonder of it held Wren fast, page after page filling with vanished steps. It was a discovery to stop the heart. But when Wren finally looked up, the day above had grown old and the island itself was still nearly blank on the paper. Wren climbed out at last toward the ridge, carrying home a beach of ancient children and leaving the coastline for the light of another morning.

On a jut of the headland stood the old signal beacon, and Wren went out to it against the wind. It was a great rusted iron brazier on a stone base, meant to hold a warning fire, with a coil of heavy chain heaped beside it. Set into the rock nearby was something finer, a carved stone dial, its face cut with grooves and marks giving the bearing out to every landmark on the coast, a compass fixed in stone so no fog could wholly hide the way. Both spoke of careful hands and long watching. Wren stood between them in the sea wind. Inspect the rusted brazier and how it once burned, or read the carved bearing dial and what it fixed.

choices.label: Inspect the rusted brazier.
Read the carved bearing dial.

nodes.body: The brazier was cold and empty, its last fire dead so long ago that only rust remained, but Wren could read how it had once worked. A blaze lit up here, high on the headland, would throw its warning far out to sea, telling ships to stand off the hidden reef below. It had saved lives once, this cold iron. And a beacon needs fuel, which meant there must have been a store nearby, tucked out of the weather, worth finding and marking on the map. But searching for it would cost time, and the day was not long. Wren could hunt along the headland for the fuel store, or give up on the dead beacon and turn back toward the ridge.

choices.label: Search along the headland for the fuel store.
Give up on the dead beacon and turn back.

nodes.body: Wren poked about the cold brazier and the rocks around it, hunting for the fuel store, and found nothing but rust and old ash and a great deal of wind. There was no way to light the thing and precious little worth drawing, and the search had swallowed a good hour for a plain dead end. A little cross with the wasted time, Wren straightened up, brushed the ash from both hands, and turned back toward the ridge. The dead beacon was barely worth a dot on the paper. Not every corner of an island rewards the looking, and a mapmaker learns, sometimes the hard way, when to cut a loss and move on.

Tucked under a low stone lintel Wren found the fuel store at last, empty now but dry, and worth every minute of the search. On the inside of the door, scrawled in chalk and pencil, was a keeper's tally of the beacon fires, and beneath it, in a hand Wren was learning to know, a last line. Gone to finish the ridge chart, it read, back by the storm. Alder had written it and walked out, expecting to return, and the storm had made a liar of the note. Wren stood in the cold little store, moved. Copy Alder's last message and cross to the ridge, or sit a while with the words that were never answered.

choices.label: Copy Alder's last message and cross to the ridge.
Sit with Alder's unanswered message.

nodes.body: Alder's scrawled line undid Wren a little, back by the storm, written by someone who fully meant to return and never did. The morning went to sitting with it in the cold fuel store, copying the words, wondering about the storm that had changed everything, the wind and the waves and the boat that came too late. It was a piece of the lost mapmaker's story, and Wren could not simply walk past it. But a story is not a survey, however much it moves you, and while Wren sat, the chart stayed as empty as the store. Home went Alder's last message at dusk, carried close, and the island's coastline left for a steadier day.

The carved dial was a gift, and Wren knelt over it with growing delight. It was a stone compass rose, cut into the living rock, with a groove and a name for every headland, reef, and peak in sight, each bearing fixed exactly and lettered in a small sure hand. Alder had spent who knew how long chiseling the whole coast into stone, so that no fog and no failing memory could ever wholly hide the way. It was the bones of a chart, laid bare and permanent. Wren could copy the dial's main bearings and cross to the ridge with them, or stay and copy every single mark on the great dial, the whole patient work of it.

choices.label: Copy the dial's bearings and cross to the ridge.
Copy every single bearing on the great dial.

nodes.body: Wren copied every bearing cut into Alder's stone dial, mark by mark, name by name, a perfect skeleton for any chart of the island, the bones handed down from one mapmaker to the next. It was genuinely useful work, the frame on which a real survey could be built. But copying it all ate the good light, and a frame is not a picture. When Wren finally rose, stiff-kneed, the sun had swung low and there was no coastline drawn between all those careful bearings. Home went Alder's stone compass, faithfully copied, and an island still empty inside its own true edges. A frame around a blank, and the day gone into it.

The wreck lay keeled over on the bar, a great old ship gone to ribs like the bones of a beached whale, its timbers black and slick with age. Wren approached with care and a kind of hush, minding the tide at the edge of sight. The flooded hold gaped open below, dim with green water and half-seen shapes. The tilted deck climbed above, a slanting ramp up toward the bow. And at the very front, a carved figurehead still leaned out over the sand, weathered but proud, keeping a watch on a sea that had already claimed her ship. Wren stood in the wreck's long shadow. Reach down into the flooded hold, or climb the tilted deck toward the bow and the figurehead.

choices.label: Reach down into the flooded hold.
Climb the tilted deck to the bow.

nodes.body: The hold was half full of green water, and dim shapes of old cargo showed under the surface, reachable if Wren waded in now while the way was clear. But Wren paused at the edge and read the light. Out on the flats it had turned silver, that first faint sheen that means the tide has begun to slide back in, and a person caught inside a wreck by rising water is a person in real and sudden trouble. The hold might hold something worth the map. It might just as easily hold a person too long. Wren could wade in quickly for one careful look and get straight out, or push deeper and trust the flooding sea to wait, which it would not.

choices.label: Wade in quickly for a look at the cargo.
Push your luck deep in the flooding hold.

nodes.body: Wren waded too deep into the wreck, chasing one more shape in the green water, just as the tide slid back across the bar. It happened fast, the way the flats always warned it would. When Wren turned to leave, the way out had narrowed to a gap of grey water between the black ribs, and for one cold heart-lurching moment it looked too small. Wren scrambled through it soaked and scared, hauling out onto the bar as the sea closed the hold behind. The lesson landed hard and stayed learned. Wren splashed to shore with the wreck only half seen and the flats left dangerously unfinished, and was simply, deeply glad to be standing on dry sand.

In the hold Wren found barrels furred thick with weed, and wedged fast among the ship's ribs, a great brass bell gone green with the sea. Wren rubbed the crust from its face and read the raised name beneath, and the reading sent a shiver up both arms. The Marigold. This was the very ship that had carried the island's first settlers, the families who came and built and, in some tellings, never sailed home. Here she lay, at last, on the bar that had wrecked her. It was a piece of the island's whole beginning. Wren could note the settler ship and wade on toward the cliff gap while the tide allowed, or stay in the wreck to read the whole story of her ending.

choices.label: Note the settler ship and wade on to the cliff gap.
Stay to read the whole story of the wreck.

nodes.body: Wren stayed and pieced the wreck's whole story together, from the green bell and the weed-furred barrels and the pattern of the broken ribs, and it came clear like a tale told in wood and brass. The Marigold, that had brought the first families to the island and then foundered here on the bar, her voyage ended a stone's throw from the shore she sought. It was a tale to make the whole Chart House fall silent. But the telling cost the tide, and Wren felt the water rising cold around both ankles before the last of it was clear. Home went a settler ship remembered whole, and the flats left a soaking blur, and Wren judged the trade a fair one.

Wren climbed the tilted deck to the bow, testing each slanting plank before trusting it, up to where the carved figurehead leaned out over the sand with a name board weathered beneath her. But the real prize was the view. From the high stern rail, Wren could see the whole pattern of the flats laid out below, every channel, every bar, every firm safe road across, clear as a chart already drawn. It was the best vantage on the whole shore. Wren could sketch the flats from up here in one commanding sweep and climb down toward the cliff gap while the tide still allowed, or stay perched at the bow to draw the weathered figurehead in loving detail instead.

choices.label: Sketch the flats from the high stern and climb down to the cliff gap.
Stay on the bow to draw the figurehead in loving detail.

nodes.body: Wren perched at the bow and drew the weathered figurehead until she seemed almost to breathe on the page, the carved guardian of a ship that never came home, every crack and salt-worn curve set down with love. It was a haunting, lovely piece of work. But the flats do not care for art, and while Wren drew, the returning tide came whispering across the sand below, cutting off the safe way with its patient speed. Wren scrambled down at last and made shore just in time, the figurehead finished in the sketchbook and the coastline still bare. A face remembered, a map unmade, and the sea rising over the bar behind to reclaim them both.

The rim of the cove was a garden of tide pools, and Wren crouched among them delighted. Each one was a jewelled little world of its own, anemones waving pink and green, tiny fish darting, crabs sidling under ledges, the whole reef edge in miniature. And low in the cliff, half hidden, a small sea cave breathed slowly in and out with the swell, murmuring of something further in. Both were worth a mapmaker's time. The pools would fix the reef edge beautifully, the safe line a boat could follow. The cave promised a deeper secret. Wren wiped salt spray from both eyes and chose. Explore the low sea cave, or study the jewelled tide pools first.

choices.label: Explore the low sea cave.
Study the jewelled tide pools.

nodes.body: The sea cave was shallow and, at its back, surprisingly dry, and Wren waded in with care, minding the slick footing. There on the rock, cut deep and sure, were initials and a date, and Wren traced them and knew the hand. Alder's. Wedged into a cleft nearby sat a small waterproof tin, and inside it a spare pencil, sharp still, and a folded scrap of paper. Wren unfolded it and read the single line written there. The way up is behind you, friend. It was a kindness left for a stranger, a hand reaching across the years to help whoever came next. Take Alder's pencil and climb the cove path, or sit a while with the kind hidden note.

choices.label: Take Alder's pencil and climb the cove path.
Sit with Alder's kind hidden note.

nodes.body: The little note, the way up is behind you, friend, felt like Alder reaching a hand clean across the years to steady a stranger, and Wren sat down with it in the breathing cave, undone by the simple kindness of it. Whoever Alder had been, they had thought of the ones who would come after, and left them help and a friendly word in a tin. It was a moment worth more than any chart. But the moment stretched, the way such moments do, and the tide breathed in and out, and the light changed. Wren climbed out at last with Alder's pencil in hand, a full heart, and a page still empty, and could not bring itself to mind.

Wren studied the jewelled tide pools one by one, and out of them read something a boat's captain would pay dearly for. The pattern of the pools mapped the reef edge exactly, and from it Wren could trace the whole run of the cove's channel, the safe deep line a vessel could follow in past the teeth of the reef to the calm water within. It was the very work Alder had begun and never finished, taken up now by another hand. Wren pencilled it in, sure and glad. Then the cut path called upward, out of the pools and toward the ridge. Add the reef edge and climb toward the ridge, or map every last pool in jewelled detail.

choices.label: Add the reef edge and climb toward the ridge.
Map every last pool in jewelled detail.

nodes.body: Wren mapped every tide pool in loving, jewelled detail, anemone by anemone, crab by crab, and the cove's reef edge came out perfect on the page, the safe channel drawn so true a captain could have steered by it blind. It was genuinely useful work, the kind that keeps ships off the rocks. But the perfect corner cost the day. Pool by pool, the hours drained, and the sun swung round, and when Wren finally climbed away from the water it was with one flawless reef in the sketchbook and a whole blank island rising above it. A beautiful edge, and no middle, and the light already leaning toward the long way home.

The side rift was hung with glowworms like a fallen sky, thousands upon thousands of tiny blue-green lights scattered across the black roof and walls. Wren stood still and simply looked, breath fogging faintly in the cool, feeling very small under all those living stars. It was one of the most beautiful things Wren had ever seen. The rift ran two ways from here. At one end it pinched down to a narrow squeeze in the rock, a tight way on through to the wider caves beyond. At the other, a still pool caught the glowing ceiling and doubled it, a place to sit beneath the light. Wren fixed the glow in memory and chose which way to take.

choices.label: Photograph the glowworms in memory and squeeze through.
Sit beneath the glowing ceiling.

nodes.body: The squeeze was a hand's breadth too narrow to be comfortable, cold rock pressing close on both shoulders, and Wren studied it before trying. Beyond it, the passage clearly opened into the wider cave, so it went somewhere worth going. A slow, careful wriggle, breathing out to make the body small, would carry through. But forcing it in a panicky rush was how a person wedged fast for a frightening minute with the rock gripping tight and no easy way back. Wren breathed slowly, judged the width against the width of both shoulders, and got ready to ease through the tight place the calm way, one careful inch at a time.

choices.label: Wriggle carefully through the squeeze.
Force it in a panicky rush.

nodes.body: Wren pushed too hard, too fast, and stuck fast in the cold squeeze, the rock gripping both shoulders with the way ahead and the way back both suddenly impossible. For one long, horrible minute panic scratched at the edges of everything. But Wren made the lungs go slow, breathed all the way out to shrink the chest, and wriggled backward, patiently, until the rock let go and spat Wren free the way in. That was quite enough of narrow places for one day. Wren sat back against the cool wall, heart steadying, and left the rift as a shaky note on the map, resolved to keep to wider caves from here on.

Wren sat down beneath the glowing ceiling and drew the rift by its own blue light, the worms strung overhead, the still pool doubling them below, a page that came out looking like a piece of the night sky brought down underground. It was quiet, patient work, and utterly lovely. When it was done, the glow showed an easy way on as well, the ground sloping off gently toward a sound Wren had been half hearing all along, the trickle and murmur of the spring somewhere ahead. Wren could follow that easy way toward the spring and the higher country, or stay a while longer lost in the glowing dark.

choices.label: Follow the easy way to the spring.
Stay lost in the glowing dark too long.

nodes.body: The glowworm rift was so beautiful that Wren lay back on the cool stone and simply watched the living stars, and time came unstuck the way it always does under the ground, where there is no sun to mark it. Minutes or hours, it was hard to say. The blue lights drifted overhead, and Wren drifted with them, emptied of hurry. When Wren finally stirred and remembered the day, the light in the world above had long since moved on without waiting. Home went a page of underground sky, careful and glowing, and an island still waiting for its map. Some ceilings, Wren decided, are worth losing an afternoon beneath.

The deer path opened on a hidden dell, so sheltered and green it felt like a secret room in the middle of the forest. Soft grass floored it, a ring of ferns walled it, and a small spring trickled up at its heart, filling the whole hollow with a low silver sound. It was the kind of place that asked you to stop and stay. A mossy carved bench sat by the water, old and inviting. From the spring, a stream slid off downhill toward wherever the water gathered. And a faint way climbed on out of the dell toward the upper ridge. Wren stood at the edge of the green room, reluctant, somehow, to break its quiet.

choices.label: Follow the dell spring downhill.
Examine the mossy carved bench.
Doze in the secret green room.

nodes.body: The dell was so peaceful that Wren meant only to rest a moment on the soft grass by the spring, and instead rested a whole hour, lulled by the trickle of the water and the green shade. Sleep crept up unasked. Wren woke with a jolt to a higher sun and a colder slant of shadow across the ferns, and the truth landed hard. The secret green room had quietly stolen the morning while Wren dozed. There was nothing for it but to hurry out, blinking, with only the dell itself drawn on the paper and far too much of the island still blank. A lovely place, and a costly nap.

Wren followed the dell spring downhill as it gathered strength, a trickle becoming a proper little stream, sliding over stones and ferns and leading gently on. The way was easy and the water sure, and Wren let it be the guide. Bit by bit the stream grew, joined by others coming down the slope, until it spilled into a wider cold pool ringed with ferns, welling up clear and quiet from the rock. This was the island's true spring, the heart of all its water, and standing over it Wren suddenly saw how the whole watershed fit together, every stream a line drawn from this one source. Wren came out at the spring, the pattern of the island clear at last.

choices.label: Come out at the spring.

nodes.body: The mossy bench was carved all over, and Wren brushed the green away to read it. Initials, dates, little scratched maps, left by mapmakers who had rested here over the years, one after another, each adding a mark to the wood. It was a guest book cut in a bench, a long quiet company of people who had loved this work before Wren was born. And there, among them, small and neat, was Alder's mark. Adding a mark of one's own felt like joining that company, taking a place in a line that stretched back and forward both. Wren traced Alder's initials, then looked at the empty grain, and at the path climbing on toward the upper ridge.

choices.label: Add your mark and climb to the upper ridge.
Read every name carved in the old bench.

nodes.body: Wren read every name and little map carved into the old bench, the whole long history of mapmakers who had rested here going back well before Alder, and copied them all down, moved by the quiet company of them. Each mark was a person, a day, a journey. The pencil filled page after page with other people's initials and other people's little maps. It was a lovely record of the ones who came before, worth keeping, worth honoring. But it was not a chart of this island, and the day slid on while Wren read the wood. The dell got drawn, the bench got copied, and the island itself stayed bare.

The loose slab lifted with a grind of stone on stone, and beneath it lay a shallow cavity in the floor. Wren peered in. There sat a small iron strongbox, cold and heavy, its lid latched with a lock gone orange with rust. On a nail nearby hung a rusted key, left for whoever might come, but the lock looked seized solid, and Wren had a feeling it would not give up easily. There was a patient way and a forceful way to meet a stubborn lock. The key might turn if worked gently, in time. Or the lock might be levered by main force, at the cost of a bent tool or a barked knuckle. Wren weighed the box, and the key, and the time.

choices.label: Try the rusted key patiently.
Lever the seized lock by main force.

nodes.body: Wren levered at the seized lock by main force, working a bar of old iron under the hasp and heaving. The lock held, and held, and then the makeshift tool bent and slipped and raked a hard line across one knuckle. Wren hissed, snatched the hand back, and looked at the graze and the still-shut box with equal frustration. Forcing it had cost a bent bar, a bleeding knuckle, and the better part of a half hour, and won nothing at all. Nursing the graze, Wren gave the strongbox up as a bad job and left the tower's last secret locked for someone with a steadier key. Some doors only open for patience, and Wren had spent the wrong coin.

The rusted key fought, then turned at last with a gritty click, and the strongbox opened on a mapmaker's treasure. A full set of fine brass drawing tools lay nested inside, pens and dividers and a little ruling edge, kept oiled and bright against the years. And on top of them lay a sealed letter, the wax unbroken, addressed in a hand Wren now knew as well as any, to whoever finishes my work. Wren lifted it out with careful fingers. The tools alone would grace any chart. But the letter was a voice reaching across the years, waiting to be heard. Take the tools and the letter and climb the stair, or break the seal and read Alder's words right now.

choices.label: Take the tools and the letter and climb the stair.
Open and read Alder's sealed letter now.

nodes.body: Wren broke the wax seal and read Alder's letter, there in the ruined tower, and it landed like a warm hand on the shoulder. It was a gentle charge to the one who would come after, to love the island and finish the map and never mind the glory of it, only the truth. Whoever you are, it ended, the work is yours now. Wren read it twice, and the reading took what was left of the good light. Home went Alder's blessing at dusk, folded close, and the brass tools, and a chart still to be drawn. It was not the ending the mate wanted, but Wren felt chosen, somehow, and that was worth a blank page.

The grassy dunes rolled behind the beach, whispering with dry marram grass, and Wren eyed their crest with a mapmaker's greed, because from up there the whole sweep of the flats would lie clear as a spread chart. A weathered marker post leaned on the highest dune, promising it was a place worth reaching. But the soft sand of a dune is exhausting to climb, sliding back a half-step for every full one, and the tide out on the flats waits for nobody, not even a tired climber with a good reason. Wren looked from the tempting crest to the shining flats and the far sea. Climb to the marker post for the great view, or wallow too long in the soft dunes trying.

choices.label: Climb to the marker post for the view.
Wallow too long in the soft dunes.

nodes.body: The soft sand slid away under every step, and the climb turned into a floundering, sweating slog that left Wren gasping and somehow no higher than before, the crest always another exhausting stride away. And while Wren struggled, the tide was already whispering back across the flats below, quiet and quick and indifferent. There is a point where a good idea becomes a bad one, and the dune had passed it. Wren gave up the crest, slithered back down defeated, and trudged along the firming sand instead. The great commanding view stayed unwon on the ridge above, and the flats went onto the paper as a hurried scribble, beaten by soft sand and a rising sea.

The marker post was a surveyor's, notched and painted long ago and faded now, and from its dune the whole pattern of the flats opened below Wren like a chart already drawn. Every channel showed, every bar, every firm safe road across the shining mud, the deep places and the shallow, all laid out plain from this one high vantage. Wren sketched fast and sure, the commanding view making easy work of what would have taken hours down among the mud. This was how a surveyor was meant to see, from height, with the whole shape at once. Wren could take the flats in this single clear view and follow the firm channels inland, or stay up on the breezy dune drawing every last channel in detail.

choices.label: Take the whole flats in one view and follow the channels in.
Stay on the breezy dune drawing every channel.

nodes.body: From the marker dune Wren drew the flats entire, every channel and bar and safe crossing set down true, a genuinely fine map of the whole shining shore, the kind that would guide a boat in safely for years. The commanding view made it a pleasure to draw. But the pleasure was the trap. It was so satisfying up there, the wind and the height and the perfect spread of the flats, that Wren stayed and stayed, perfecting a corner that was already done. When Wren finally climbed down, the flats were beautifully finished and the rest of the island lay untouched, the day spent on one flawless shore and no time left for the others.
